concepts/stack-ranking-and-team-incentiv.title: stack-ranking-and-team-incentives
concepts/stack-ranking-and-team-incentiv.content:
Stack Ranking and Team Incentives
Stack ranking is the performance-management practice of ranking people relative to one another, often with special consequences for the bottom of the distribution. In the currently ingested sources, it is less important as a review mechanic than as an incentive system that changes how teams behave.
Cantrill’s Critique
Bryan Cantrill gives the strongest critique in the Peterman Pod episode. His claim is that forced ranking with bottom-slice exits can teach teammates to see one another as adversaries. If someone has to be low-ranked, then another person’s weakness can become useful protection rather than a team problem to solve.
The practical damage is not only emotional unfairness. It is structural. A system intended to identify weak performance can reward managers for carrying obvious weak performers as sacrificial ballast, while making high performers less willing to think in team-first terms.
Evans’s Different Emphasis
Ethan Evans presents a more mixed performance-management picture. In his Amazon episode, he says pressure to identify low performers can force managers to have conversations they might otherwise avoid, but he also acknowledges the unfairness that appears when managers need to fill a quota-like list.
This creates a useful unresolved tension. Evans is worried about managers avoiding accountability. Cantrill is worried about ranking systems corrupting the team itself. The wiki should keep those as different claims rather than flattening them into a single “performance management is good or bad” conclusion.
Link to Promotion Culture
This concept extends Big-Tech Promotion Culture. Promotion theater is about work being chosen because it creates a good advancement narrative. Stack-ranking distortion is about teammates and managers changing behavior because relative ranking has survival consequences.
Both are incentive failures. In both cases, the review system stops merely evaluating work and starts shaping the work environment in ways that may reduce engineering quality.
Backlinks

concepts/statistics-book-recommendations.title: statistics-book-recommendations
concepts/statistics-book-recommendations.content:
Statistics Book Recommendations
This page reconstructs John Myles White’s Peterman Pod statistics reading recommendations. The episode transcript names authors and describes why he trusts them; the episode show notes provide the most concrete book-title evidence.
Source-Backed Recommendations

All of Statistics: A Concise Course in Statistical Inference by Larry Wasserman. The show notes link this as the concrete Wasserman title, and White says Wasserman’s books are unusually honest about assumptions, claims, and limits.
Foundations of Agnostic Statistics by Peter M. Aronow and Benjamin T. Miller. The show notes link this as the Peter Aronow book, and White describes it as less math-heavy than Wasserman and especially useful for social scientists or practical empirical work.

Speculative Reconstruction
White says to buy and read anything by Larry Wasserman. The most likely first Wasserman book is All of Statistics, because the episode show notes link that title directly.
A plausible second Wasserman title is All of Nonparametric Statistics, because it is another major Wasserman textbook in the same “All of” series and would fit White’s broad “anything by Wasserman” recommendation. This is an inferred addition, not a title White is known from this source to have named.
Active Reading
The user has started rereading All of Statistics, confirming it as the current primary Wasserman reading target. The first partial ingestion covers the preface and Chapter 1, “Probability,” where Wasserman frames probability as reasoning from a data-generating process to outcomes and statistical inference as the inverse problem.
Why These Fit White’s Criteria
White’s stated criterion is intellectual honesty. He praises authors who state assumptions clearly, say what follows from those assumptions, and refuse to imply stronger practical claims than the math supports.
That criterion matters for software engineers because A/B test tools can make statistical decisions look like UI state. White’s Deltoid example shows the failure mode: teams can treat barely significant or barely non-significant results as goal machinery rather than as uncertain evidence.
Reading Order

All of Statistics if the goal is a mathematically serious, compact overview of probability and statistical inference.
Foundations of Agnostic Statistics if the goal is empirical research, social-science-style inference, causal reasoning, or practical caution about assumptions.
All of Nonparametric Statistics only after deciding the nonparametric branch is relevant; this is a speculative extension from White’s general Wasserman endorsement.

Backlinks

concepts/technology-and-worker-power.title: technology-and-worker-power
concepts/technology-and-worker-power.content:
Technology and Worker Power
Technology and worker power is the core institutional thesis from Power and Progress: productivity growth becomes shared prosperity only when workers have enough bargaining, political, and social power to shape technology’s direction and claim part of its gains.
Against Automatic Progress
The book rejects the idea that new machines automatically raise wages. Automation can increase output per worker while reducing the need for additional workers. When that happens, average productivity rises but worker marginal productivity may stagnate or fall.
The result depends on the direction of technology. Tools that create new tasks, raise worker capability, and increase demand for labor can support shared prosperity. Tools that mainly replace workers, monitor them, or weaken their bargaining position can raise inequality even when they look like progress.
Institutions Matter
Worker gains require institutions that turn productivity into bargaining power. In the book’s historical account, shared prosperity appeared when unions, democratic voice, public health regulation, social insurance, competition policy, and broader civic pressure constrained owners and managers.
This is why the postwar decades looked different from early industrialization. The technology was not magically benevolent. It was embedded in institutions that created new tasks and forced rent sharing.
AI Extension
The book treats current AI as a test of the same principle. AI can be used to augment workers, create new tasks, improve information flows, and build useful platforms. But the dominant path currently rewards automation, surveillance, scheduling control, targeted advertising, and data extraction.
That means AI governance is not only about model capability. It is also about who gets a voice in deployment decisions: workers affected by monitoring, users affected by data collection, citizens affected by misinformation, and communities affected by labor-market restructuring.
Practical Test
A worker-power lens asks:

Does this technology raise worker marginal productivity or merely reduce headcount?
Does it create new meaningful tasks or deskill existing ones?
Does it increase worker autonomy or make monitoring more intrusive?
Do workers have a say in deployment and redesign?
Are productivity gains shared through wages, benefits, training, or safer conditions?
Does the tool strengthen democratic voice or concentrate agenda-setting power?

Relationship to Machine Usefulness
Machine Usefulness is the technical direction that fits this institutional model. Useful machines complement human capability. Worker power is what makes that direction more likely, because firms otherwise have strong incentives to use the same technologies for cost cutting and control.
AI Exponential Extension
Policy on the AI Exponential adds a policy-builder’s version of the same concern. Dario Amodei argues that AI could create rapid growth and serious labor displacement at the same time, making the central problem less about producing growth and more about sharing benefits, slowing harmful transitions, and preserving purpose and agency.
The tension is that redistribution after displacement is not the same as worker power before deployment. A Post-AI World agenda needs both: mechanisms that share AI-created wealth and institutions that let workers and citizens shape whether AI is used for complementarity, monitoring, replacement, or new task creation.
Frontier Ecosystem Extension
A Frontier Without An Ecosystem Is Not Stable adds a related but different power problem: whether a few model providers capture the value of every industry’s accumulated expertise. Nadella’s answer is firm-owned Organizational AI Learning Loops, where companies retain private evals, traces, knowledge bases, and domain-specific token capital.
That helps with company sovereignty, but it does not automatically solve worker power. If employees’ tacit knowledge becomes training signal for firm-owned systems, the worker-power question becomes who consents, who governs the loop, and who shares in the compounding value.
Employee Activity As Training Data
Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization? gives this concern a concrete workplace form. Orosz reports that Meta attempted to collect employee keystrokes and mouse activity for AI training data, while also reassigning engineers into data-labeling and RLHF work.
This is a weak worker-power pattern: low consent, limited voice, high monitoring, and unclear sharing of the value created from employee judgment. It also shows why firm-owned token capital can still be extractive. The firm may become less dependent on external model providers while workers become more exposed to surveillance and deskilling.

concepts/the-culture.title: the-culture
concepts/the-culture.content:
The Culture
The central fictional civilization of Iain M. Banks’s Culture series. First encountered in Consider Phlebas (1987).
Core Characteristics

Post-scarcity: No money (“a crude, over-complicated and inefficient form of rationing”). All material needs met by technology. Energy drawn from fusion, annihilation, stellar taps, or the Grid.
Machine-governed: Run by hyper-intelligent AIs called Minds. Minds inhabit ships and orbital habitats. Humans live alongside machines as equals, but the Minds are vastly more capable.
Atheist and hedonistic: No religion, no empire-building, no need to colonize or exploit. Citizens live long, pleasurable lives.
Morally purposeful via Contact: The Culture’s “secular evangelism” — the Contact Section finds, studies, and sometimes covertly intervenes in the development of less advanced civilizations.

Why the Culture Fought the Idirans
From the appendix “Reasons: the Culture” (source, p. 358–359):

It was, the Culture knew from the start, a religious war in the fullest sense. The Culture went to war to safeguard its own peace of mind: no more.

The Idirans never threatened the Culture with conquest or territory. But they threatened the Culture’s purpose — the reason it could justify its pampered existence. Backing down would have destroyed the Culture’s spirit. It had no choice but to fight.
Physical Structures

































StructureDescriptionOrbitalEnormous ring habitat, matter-cheap, habitable inside surfaceGeneral Systems Vehicle (GSV)Largest ships; city-sized; carry millions of inhabitants; city-ships in spaceGeneral Contact Unit (GCU)Smaller ships; used by Contact for first contact and diplomacyLimited Systems Vehicle (LSV)Mid-size ship classRange class GSVLarge GSV variantRockAsteroid-type habitat
Ships and Their Names
Culture ships choose their own names, typically ironic, jokey, or philosophically pointed. Examples from Consider Phlebas:

Clear Air Turbulence (mercenary ship, non-Culture)
The Hand of God 137 (Idiran light cruiser — contrasts with Culture naming style)
No More Mr Nice Guy (Culture GSV where the Mind was repaired)
Irregular Apocalypse (GSV, served by drone Unaha-Closp)
Profit Margin (LSV)
Prosthetic Conscience (GCU, revisited Schar’s World post-war)
Nervous Energy (Mountain class GCU)
Determinist (System class GSV, post-war)
Bora Horza Gobuchul (Range class GSV — the rescued Mind’s chosen name, in honour of its captor/enemy)

Contact and Special Circumstances
Contact is the section of Culture society that interfaces with other civilizations. Within Contact is Special Circumstances — the covert operations arm, handling interventions that cannot be done openly. Balveda works for Contact (implied to be Special Circumstances).
From The Player of Games: SC uses individuals as “game-pieces” without their knowledge. Gurgeh is recruited, trained, and sent to Azad specifically because SC believed he could win — and that his winning would destabilize the Empire. He is never told the stakes. SC’s position: “A guilty system recognises no innocents.” The Culture’s apparent non-interventionism is a PR position; SC intervenes covertly and routinely. When asked whether SC would really have invaded Azad if Gurgeh lost, the SC drone answers: “I have no idea. Not in my brief; no need to know. It doesn’t matter; he believed it was true.”
Drug glands: Culture humans are genofixed with the ability to internally produce up to ~300 different compounds. Sharp Blue is a cognitive-enhancer/abstraction-modifier used by game-players; it “made what seemed complicated simple.” Possession of drug glands disqualifies players from gaining imperial rank in the Empire of Azad.
The Culture’s Relationship to Organic Life
Protagonist Horza’s critique: the Culture has made organic life irrelevant — humans are pets of their machines. The Minds are the real Culture; the humans just live in it. Banks presents this critique seriously but the novel’s outcome implies it’s ultimately wrong, or at least that it’s not a sufficient reason to side against the Culture.
War Statistics (Idiran-Culture War)
See Idiran-Culture War Timeline.

concepts/vagabondage-in-quicksilver.title: vagabondage-in-quicksilver
concepts/vagabondage-in-quicksilver.content:
Vagabondage in Quicksilver
In Quicksilver, vagabondage is not only poverty or criminality. In the ingested Book Two material, it is a mobile social system made from execution crowds, thieves, deserters, actors, smugglers, displaced peasants, plague refugees, escaped serfs, rat-catchers, camp followers, and informal brokers.
The Devil’s Poor
Jack Shaftoe distinguishes between God’s poor, who can be treated as deserving charity, and the Devil’s Poor, who are treated as irreformable. The label is social and political as much as moral: it marks people who refuse mastery, cannot be fitted into parish charity, and are therefore controlled by whipping, branding, cropping, hanging, imprisonment, or expulsion.
Information Network
Jack treats Vagabond camps as libraries of spoken intelligence. He uses fish, spectacle, and shared outcast status to draw people out and learn where he and Eliza can survive winter.
This network is not formal, literate, or centralized, but it reaches across roads, borders, fairs, armies, prisons, and cities. It is one of Jack’s real powers: he cannot read books, but he can read people, routes, punishments, and risks.
Performance and Survival
Jack’s childhood trade at Newgate already combines commerce, performance, and death. He and Bob turn hanging assistance into a service, selling it through grotesque theater.
The same pattern follows Jack across Europe. He survives by playing roles: soldier, servant, tinker, messenger, cripple, bodyguard, and gentleman’s retainer. These roles are not stable identities; they are temporary costumes that let him pass through systems built to exclude or punish him.
Limit
Book Two ends by showing the limit of Jack’s vagabond intelligence. On God’s Wounds, he tries to turn shipboard violence into reputation, but language and circumstance fail him. The Barbary capture converts the king of vagabonds into a galley slave, meaning that mobility finally collapses back into coerced labor.

concepts/viewstamped-replication.title: viewstamped-replication
concepts/viewstamped-replication.content:
Viewstamped Replication
Viewstamped Replication is a replication protocol discussed by Barbara Liskov in her Peterman Pod interview. In the episode, she describes it as work on keeping data available and correct across multiple sites despite failures, as long as enough nodes and the network remain functioning.
Core Idea
The protocol uses views and viewstamps to reason about which replica group is current and which nodes have the newest state after failures. The goal is to support replicated service behavior without allowing stale or inconsistent state to become authoritative.
Relationship to Paxos
Liskov says Viewstamped Replication and Leslie Lamport’s Paxos were developed independently and are essentially the same idea, with differences in details. This wiki should treat that as an interview-source claim until the original papers are ingested and compared directly.
Backlinks

Barbara Liskov
Data Abstraction
Peterman Pod: Turing Award Winner Barbara Liskov

concepts/zero-knowledge-proofs.title: zero-knowledge-proofs
concepts/zero-knowledge-proofs.content:
Zero-Knowledge Proofs
Zero-knowledge proofs are interactive proofs where a prover convinces a verifier that a claim is true without revealing the underlying proof, witness, or secret beyond the truth of the claim.
Why They Matter
In Avi Wigderson’s Peterman Pod episode, the cryptographic motivation is correct behavior with secrets. A participant may need to prove that a public value was generated correctly, or that a protocol step was followed, without revealing the secret inputs that make the step valid.
The episode’s example is public-key-style reasoning: someone may need to convince others that a number was generated by multiplying two primes, while not revealing the primes themselves.
Interactive Proof Frame
Wigderson situates zero-knowledge proofs inside interactive proofs. Instead of sending a static proof once, a prover and verifier can interact. The verifier may use randomness, and the protocol can tolerate a small probability that a false claim slips through. Repeating the interaction can make that soundness error extremely small.
Zero knowledge adds a privacy condition: the verifier learns that the claim is true but gains no useful knowledge about the witness.
Graph Coloring Intuition
The episode explains the idea through graph coloring. A prover can commit to a randomly permuted coloring, and the verifier asks to inspect only one edge. If both endpoint colors differ every time, confidence grows that the graph has a valid coloring. Because the colors are randomly permuted each round and only a tiny local check is exposed, the verifier does not learn the coloring itself.
Wigderson then uses NP-completeness to generalize the idea. If a proof or witness can be reduced to a graph-coloring-style witness, then proving the graph-coloring instance in zero knowledge transfers the zero-knowledge property to the original claim.
Caveats
The episode frames zero-knowledge proofs as probabilistic interactive systems. Completeness can be made exact for honest true claims, but soundness against false claims has a small error probability that can be driven down rather than made literally zero.
This page is currently grounded only in the Peterman Pod interview. The original Goldwasser-Micali-Rackoff and Goldreich-Micali-Wigderson papers should be ingested before treating this as a primary-source history of zero knowledge.
Backlinks

Avi Wigderson
Peterman Pod: Avi Wigderson on P vs NP, Zero-Knowledge, and Quantum Computation
Computational Complexity Theory
Quantum Computation

people/andrew-curran.title: andrew-curran
people/andrew-curran.content:
Andrew Curran
Andrew Curran enters this wiki through the X Article The Window Has Closed, a 2026-06-15 argument about Fable/Mythos, frontier-model acceleration, compute chokepoints, and national AI sovereignty.
The source page identifies him as Andrew Curran, @AndrewCurran_. His profile describes him as an AI writer.
Relevance
Curran is useful here as a voice for the frontier-sovereignty alarm: the idea that access to powerful foreign AI models is not enough if a state cannot build, inspect, and keep using frontier-grade systems.
His article is especially relevant beside What it feels like to work with Mythos, which describes the user-facing experience of Fable, and A Frontier Without An Ecosystem Is Not Stable, which describes a firm-level version of AI sovereignty.

people/austen-mcdonald.content:
Austen McDonald
Austen McDonald is presented in the March 16, 2026 Peterman Pod episode as a former Meta hiring committee member who led mobile hiring and conducted hundreds of interviews.
In This Wiki

Peterman Pod: Austen McDonald on Senior+ Engineering Hiring

Source-Backed Notes
McDonald says senior-engineer hiring committees look for evidence of scope, influence, communication, and organizational operating level. His Meta examples emphasize the behavioral interview as a major leveling signal for senior and higher candidates.
He also distinguishes referrals from replacement evidence. Referrals can help candidates receive additional consideration, especially in borderline situations, but the episode does not describe referrals as overriding a clear engineering no-hire consensus.

people/avi-wigderson.title: avi-wigderson
people/avi-wigderson.content:
Avi Wigderson
Avi Wigderson is presented in the June 1, 2026 Peterman Pod episode as a theoretical computer scientist whose work spans computational complexity theory, randomness, zero-knowledge proofs, and quantum computation.
Key Ideas From The Episode
Wigderson frames P vs NP as a question about the relationship between finding and checking. In his explanation, NP contains problems where proposed solutions can be efficiently recognized, while P contains problems that can be efficiently solved. The unresolved question asks whether efficient recognition implies efficient search.
He also treats randomness as a computational resource rather than a simple physical property. Random bits have cost and quality, and pseudorandomness depends on what computational observer is trying to distinguish the sequence from truly random bits.
His zero-knowledge discussion presents interactive proofs as a way for a prover to convince a verifier without revealing the underlying witness. His quantum computation discussion treats quantum algorithms as a new model of computation that both threatens classical public-key assumptions and opens new complexity-theory techniques.
Research Notes
Wigderson describes theoretical computer science as living between mathematics and computer science: it uses theorem-proving methods, but its models are motivated by computation, resources, adversaries, algorithms, and natural processes.
His advice for early researchers is to sample different areas and work on problems they enjoy most, because affinity often reveals where talent and persistence are strongest.
Backlinks

Peterman Pod: Avi Wigderson on P vs NP, Zero-Knowledge, and Quantum Computation

people/barbara-liskov.title: barbara-liskov
people/barbara-liskov.content:
Barbara Liskov
Barbara Liskov is a computer scientist and Turing Award winner. In the Peterman Pod episode, she discusses data abstraction, CLU, distributed systems, Viewstamped Replication, Dijkstra’s goto letter, and the career path that took her from mathematics into programming languages and systems research.
Key Ideas From The Episode
Liskov presents data abstraction as a response to the software crisis: large programs needed modules whose implementations could be hidden behind clear operations and specifications. She connects that work to CLU and to later language influence.
She also describes moving into distributed computing after reading Bob Kahn’s vision of programs spread across networked machines. That work led to Argus and Viewstamped Replication, a replication protocol she describes as independently developed and closely related to Paxos.
Career Notes
Liskov says she entered computing partly by contingency: Princeton did not admit women to the graduate math program at the time, and her best job offer after Berkeley was a programming job. Later, when she did not get the faculty offer she wanted after her PhD, she returned to MITRE, which gave her time to shift from AI to systems before academia opened more fully to women.
Her research advice - choose problems that are important, soluble, and matched to the researcher - now supports Choosing What to Work On.
Backlinks

people/bjarne-stroustrup.title: bjarne-stroustrup
people/bjarne-stroustrup.content:
Bjarne Stroustrup
Bjarne Stroustrup is the creator of C++ and a former Bell Labs researcher. In the Peterman Pod episode, he discusses the origin of C++, Bell Labs, Dennis Ritchie, language-design tradeoffs, memory safety, standards committees, abstraction and performance, AI-generated code, and C++ education.
Key Ideas From The Episode
Stroustrup presents C++ as a response to needing high-level abstraction and low-level performance in one language. He frames language design as problem-first engineering: a new language should solve a real problem that existing tools cannot solve adequately.
That problem-first framing is now linked into Choosing What to Work On.
He also resists treating C++ as only object-oriented. The episode emphasizes reuse of existing C and Fortran software, stronger type systems, generic programming, RAII, and abstractions that should not impose unnecessary runtime costs.
On memory safety, Stroustrup argues for modern C++ idioms rather than C-style raw-pointer habits. His preferred direction includes vectors, spans, RAII, hardened libraries, static analysis, profiles, and education.
Bell Labs and Stewardship
Stroustrup describes Bell Labs Research Culture as unusually effective because it combined world-class practical engineering with room for excellent people to choose interesting work. At the same time, his C++ story is full of constraints: small machines, existing users, compatibility, committees, and limited tooling.
His advice to his younger self focuses on governance and timing: he would have pushed harder on problematic conversions, waited for stronger standard-library foundations, and tried to create a smaller steering structure for language evolution.
Backlinks

people/bryan-cantrill.title: bryan-cantrill
people/bryan-cantrill.content:
Bryan Cantrill
Bryan Cantrill is presented in the Peterman Pod episode as a former distinguished engineer at Sun Microsystems and a co-founder of Oxide Computer Company.
In This Wiki
Cantrill’s episode is useful for three wiki threads:

Stack Ranking and Team Incentives: forced ranking can distort team behavior and make teammates adversarial.
Big-Tech Promotion Culture: promotion systems can become harmful when titles, rankings, and narratives overpower meaningful technical work.
Engineering Career Growth: career growth is healthier when it follows important work and intrinsic motivation rather than title fixation.
Choosing What to Work On: intrinsic technical conviction and customer-facing problems should beat title fixation and hype.

Notes
Cantrill’s central career pattern is anti-title-chasing but not anti-ambition. He argues for choosing meaningful technical work, building strong teams, and letting recognition follow from durable contribution.
His Oxide story also turns a hiring failure into an operating principle: make hiring more rigorous, values-oriented, and writing-intensive because team quality is the product’s foundation.

people/daniel-waterhouse.title: daniel-waterhouse
people/daniel-waterhouse.content:
Daniel Waterhouse
Fictional natural philosopher in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver. This page is a focused person note; fuller Volume One chronology is in Quicksilver Plot.
Role in Quicksilver
In 1713, Daniel is an elderly Fellow-adjacent natural philosopher living in Massachusetts. He has founded the Massachusetts Bay Colony Institute of Technologickal Arts and is trying to build the Logic Mill, an automatic computation device using binary-numbered cards and mechanical pushrods.
Princess Caroline of Ansbach sends Enoch Root to bring him back to England, where he is expected to help settle or heal the Newton-Leibniz calculus dispute. Daniel initially refuses because he has a wife, Faith, a son, Godfrey, and a new life in New England. The financial insurance arranged for his family makes the voyage difficult to refuse.
Early Life and Formation
Daniel was raised by Drake Waterhouse, a militant Puritan who intended Daniel for a clerical life and expected the Apocalypse in 1666. Daniel’s early university life at Trinity College is shaped by Restoration violence, religious anxiety, and his own failure to report the Earl of Upnor’s killing of a Puritan scholar.
Daniel’s friendship with Isaac Newton becomes the central intellectual relationship of his youth. He rooms with Newton, helps keep him alive through periods of obsessive work, assists optical experiments, and witnesses Newton’s early thinking about sundials, colors, fluxions, gravity, and orbital motion.
Royal Society
During the plague years, Daniel works with John Wilkins and Robert Hooke at Epsom and later in London. He helps with Wilkins’s Universal Character / Philosophical Language project, observes Hooke’s experimental culture, and later presents Newton’s reflecting telescope to the Royal Society.
Daniel is both drawn to and repelled by the Royal Society. He recognizes its discoveries and its new language of experiment, but he also sees its cruelty, social absurdity, and political vulnerability.
Family
Drake Waterhouse is Daniel’s father; Drake’s Puritan politics and apocalyptic expectations dominate Daniel’s childhood. Drake dies during the Great Fire of London after refusing to leave his house, which King Charles II orders destroyed as part of a firebreak.
Daniel’s older Waterhouse relatives are tied to commerce, goldsmith banking, real estate, and post-Fire London development. Daniel sees their world of money as powerful but opaque, a kind of dark science outside his competence.
In 1713 Massachusetts, Daniel is married to Faith Waterhouse and has a young son, Godfrey Waterhouse. Godfrey’s name appears to honor Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz.
Backlinks

Quicksilver
Quicksilver Plot
Logic Mill
Newton-Leibniz Priority Dispute
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz

people/dario-amodei.title: dario-amodei
people/dario-amodei.content:
Dario Amodei
Dario Amodei enters the wiki through Policy on the AI Exponential, a June 2026 essay on AI policy, safety regulation, labor displacement, scientific acceleration, civil liberties, and geopolitics.
The current page should be treated as source-backed only for what the ingested essay itself states or implies. It is not a full biography.
Current Source Role
In the essay, Amodei writes as an AI lab leader arguing that frontier AI has become strategically consequential faster than ordinary policy processes can handle. The essay is useful to the wiki because it combines technical AI-risk claims with a wider institutional agenda: public-safety regulation, macroeconomic sharing, downstream science policy, checks on state and corporate power, and democratic coordination.

people/daron-acemoglu.content:
Daron Acemoglu
Daron Acemoglu is presented in the Power and Progress ebook metadata as an MIT Institute Professor of Economics whose research focuses on prosperity, poverty, technology, economic growth, employment, and inequality.
In This Wiki
Acemoglu’s coauthored argument in Power and Progress is useful for the wiki’s labor, institutions, and AI threads. The book treats technological direction as a political-economic choice rather than a neutral engineering sequence.
Its most important contribution here is the connection between Technology and Worker Power and Machine Usefulness: workers and citizens benefit from technology only when institutions give them voice over how tools are designed, deployed, and governed.

people/eliza.title: eliza
people/eliza.content:
Eliza
Fictional Qwghlmian-born former slave, trader, cipher correspondent, and political operator in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver. This page is still a focused character note, not a full replacement for the complete Volume One timeline.
Origin and Captivity
Eliza says she was born in Qwghlm and captured as a child by Barbary Corsairs while with her mother. Her memories of Qwghlm are filtered through childish misunderstanding and later correction: what she remembers as local innocence often carries the harsher logic of wrecking, raiding, and survival.
After capture, Eliza and her mother pass through North African and Ottoman slavery. Eliza’s account emphasizes language, harem politics, bargaining, sexual knowledge, and the Society of Britannic Abductees founded by her mother. The still-unidentified powerful “Personage” on a pirate ship remains unresolved.
Rescue at Vienna
Jack finds Eliza in a Turkish mine under Vienna, where Janissaries are executing women from the Grand Vizier’s harem after the Ottoman defeat. Jack’s intervention saves her life, though the scene quickly shifts from rescue romance into negotiation, suspicion, and transactional partnership.
Eliza leaves Vienna disguised as a wounded male officer, riding with Jack on Turk and carrying hidden silk. She immediately begins turning survival into strategy.
Commercial Intelligence
Eliza repeatedly sees market structure before Jack does. She rejects selling ostrich plumes cheaply in thieves’ markets and pushes toward Leipzig, where luxury buyers and financial instruments can create a higher return.
At Leipzig and Amsterdam, she grasps that silk, plumes, coin, mine shares, bills of exchange, quicksilver, and information can be converted into one another. This makes commerce her route out of slave status and into agency.
Leibniz, Cipher, and Politics
Eliza meets Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz in Leipzig and later corresponds with him. He teaches her a binary-based cipher, connecting her practical intrigue to the same formal-symbolic world that includes Wilkins’s cryptography, Leibnizian calculation, and Daniel Waterhouse’s Logic Mill.
In the Hague, Jean Antoine de Mesmes, comte d’Avaux, introduces Eliza into court politics. She meets Mary of Orange and the Duke of Monmouth, and she also works with Gomer Bolstrood on procurement of lead, saltpeter, and gunpowder for a still partly hidden political project.
Book Two ends by moving Eliza from market agency into direct French danger. She leaves Amsterdam under French diplomatic escort and is violently separated from her party, setting up the titled, court-facing intelligence role that dominates the later Volume One material.
Late Volume One
Book Three turns Eliza into a titled intelligence and finance actor: Countess de la Zeur to the French, Duchess of Qwghlm to the Dutch/English orbit. She manages assets for French nobles, helps engineer the false “Fall of Batavia” market operation, studies slavery and Abigail Frome through the Monmouth aftermath, and becomes entangled with d’Avaux, Rossignol, d’Arcachon, William of Orange, Princess Eleanor, and young Caroline.
Her late correspondence reveals a split between public story and private truth. Her baby is publicly dead but privately alive under refugee cover; the father is Rossignol rather than d’Arcachon. The episode turns Eliza’s body, title, ciphers, and relationships into contested political property.
Interpretation
Eliza’s central pattern is conversion: captivity into education, costume into rank, language into access, commodity knowledge into leverage, title into political cover, and sexual vulnerability into political maneuvering. Volume One clarifies more of her Dutch/French role but does not resolve her later arc.
Backlinks

people/enoch-root.title: enoch-root
people/enoch-root.content:
Enoch Root
Recurring Neal Stephenson character appearing in both Quicksilver and Cryptonomicon.
Quicksilver
In the 1713 frame, Enoch Root, also called Enoch the Red, arrives in Boston after crossing from Europe. He witnesses a witch hanging, meets young Ben Franklin, and uses a sealed letter from Princess Caroline of Ansbach to locate Daniel Waterhouse at the Massachusetts Bay Colony Institute of Technologickal Arts.
Enoch is presented as an unusually durable, widely traveled natural philosopher and alchemist. He carries instruments, flasks of quicksilver, books in classical and occult languages, weapons, and European political knowledge. Harvard men recognize him as a Fellow of the Royal Society, and Daniel treats him as an old acquaintance. Enoch’s mission is to persuade Daniel to return to England and help with the Newton-Leibniz priority dispute.
Earlier flashbacks in the ingested portion show Enoch in the 1650s observing Isaac Newton as a schoolboy in Grantham, dealing in alchemical materials such as sulfur and antimony, and acting less as a merchant than as a messenger among alchemists and natural philosophers. In 1670, he demonstrates phosphorus at court after extracting it from urine; this ties him to the alchemical search for the Philosophic Mercury.
Book Two shows Enoch in Bockboden and the Harz mining world, near Leibniz’s Brunswick-Luneburg project. He appears in an apothecary shop when Jack is looking for help for Eliza, gives practical medical advice, and later teaches Jack how quicksilver, salt, copper dross, heat, washing, and distillation can extract silver from lower-grade ore. This makes Enoch not merely an occult alchemist but a practical metallurgical operator whose knowledge gives Leibniz leverage over mine-masters.
Cryptonomicon
In the WWII thread, Enoch Root first appears to Bobby Shaftoe on Guadalcanal under the name Red, gives last rites in Latin, tends Shaftoe, and introduces himself as Enoch Root, “Brother.” He later turns up as Lieutenant Enoch Root, a chaplain attached to Detachment 2702.
Root’s Detachment 2702 role is intentionally odd. He is a priest, medical doctor, rifle-capable field operator, Morse-code user, and code-aware intermediary. He works with Bobby Shaftoe, Lawrence Waterhouse, and Goto Dengo, helps manage cover operations, treats wounds, and participates in the final Arethusa deception in Manila. He says he is not a mathematician but can do calculations Dengo asks him to do.
Cryptonomicon also supplies biographical clues: Root’s mother is German and a medical missionary, his father is Dutch, and he knows German, Italian, Latin, Morse code, and field medicine. Shaftoe judges that Root is not simply working for either side in the war, but for a “Higher Power”; this is Shaftoe’s interpretation, not a confirmed institutional allegiance.
One important ambiguity: Cryptonomicon does not merely imply that Enoch is long-lived. In the WWII Sweden sequence, he is shot under the collarbone, undergoes emergency surgery, and the narration says he dies with Rudolf von Hacklheber, Bobby Shaftoe, and the Swedish doctor present. Shaftoe then watches the doctor prepare Enoch’s death certificate.
Enoch reappears within the WWII timeline itself, not only in the modern plot. In the CRIBS chapter, Lawrence Waterhouse watches Bobby Shaftoe’s funeral at the U.S. military cemetery in Makati and sees Enoch at the graveside with Rudy von Hacklheber, Günter Bischoff, and Goto Dengo. Enoch is also active in Manila around the Arethusa communications that lead to the funeral rendezvous.
In the modern thread, Enoch is again present, apparently unchanged enough to make his nature ambiguous. He advises Randy Waterhouse, is connected with the Pontifex/Solitaire cryptosystem, helps in the Philippines around Golgotha, treats Amy Shaftoe’s wound, kills Andrew Loeb when Loeb attacks Randy and Amy, and brings the Archbishop of Manila to the final Golgotha ceremony.
In a Manila jail conversation with Randy, Enoch gives an explicit theory of  Metis. He identifies Athena with metis, craft, technology, and the defensive intelligence civilization needs against Ares-like violence. This framework becomes a moral lens for the novel’s codebreaking, weapons, data haven, and Golgotha plots.
Interpretation
Primary-source facts: Enoch appears in both the 17th/18th-century Quicksilver material and the WWII/1990s Cryptonomicon plot; both books present him as unusually long-lived, religiously and alchemically inflected, medically capable, and cryptographically useful.
Inference: Enoch functions as a cross-era connective figure between alchemy, religion, cryptography, computation, war, and finance. His certified death in Sweden, reappearance at Shaftoe’s funeral in Makati, and later 1990s presence make the unresolved question stronger than simple longevity; the current sources leave open whether this is resurrection, alchemical intervention, mistaken death, or deliberate mythic ambiguity.

people/erika-pullum-swartz.content:
Erika Pullum (Swartz)
Erika Pullum (Swartz) is the author of Better with Data. In the ingested article Management Seat Time, she describes leaving a management role leading a large team to join Aula Education as a senior analytics engineer.
Ideas In This Wiki
Pullum (Swartz)‘s main contribution in the current wiki is the concept of Management Seat Time: the management judgment and pattern recognition that can only be built by doing management work over time.
Her article also treats the move from management back to technical work as legitimate. Management and engineering are different tracks with different skill profiles, and losing passion for management can be a valid signal to return to individual-contributor work.

people/ethan-evans.title: ethan-evans
people/ethan-evans.content:
Ethan Evans
Ethan Evans is presented in the Peterman Pod episodes as a former Amazon VP and current career coach who advises people on corporate politics, management, promotion, and influence.
In This Wiki
Ingested episodes:

Amazon VP On Promotions, Getting Fired Twice, Working With Bezos, published August 4, 2025
Amazon VP Reveals Everything He’s Seen In Corporate Politics, published March 30, 2026

Ideas From The Episode
Evans argues that corporate politics often follows from incentive design rather than from individual villainy. If promotion systems reward scope, leaders seek scope. If impact is hard to measure, headcount becomes a tempting proxy. If reorgs are already happening, leaders use them to solve business, retention, promotion, and performance-management problems at the same time.
He also argues that the social techniques of influence and manipulation overlap. His dividing line is motive: the same executive-presence, backchanneling, narrative, and leverage skills can either help an organization coordinate or help a self-serving operator capture resources.
The earlier career-story episode adds the autobiographical basis for this view. Evans says he was fired twice during the dot-com era partly because of abrasive soft skills, then deliberately learned to ask questions, build alliances, and influence without direct authority. He later describes Amazon promotions as a mix of impact, narrative, process literacy, timing, and manager advocacy.

Ethan Mollick
Ethan Mollick is the credited author of the One Useful Thing article What it feels like to work with Mythos.
In that article, Mollick reports early access to Claude 5 Fable and describes a shift from hands-on AI operation toward AI Commissioning: giving an ambitious brief, letting the model execute through long and partly hidden workflows, and then judging or correcting the finished artifact.
Wiki Relevance

Provides a firsthand account of working with a more autonomous coding and research model.
Sharpens the question of how much of the AI work process should remain observable to the human user.
Connects long-horizon model execution to engineering judgment, review, and apprenticeship.

Gergely Orosz
Gergely Orosz writes and hosts The Pragmatic Engineer, which this wiki uses for engineering practice, software-organization reporting, and technical interviews.
In This Wiki
The ingested Martin Kleppmann episode is useful because Orosz frames Designing Data-Intensive Applications as a rare technical text that became a shared engineering reference across companies and seniority levels. His questions focus on why the book worked, what changed enough to justify a second edition, and how research ideas move into everyday engineering practice.
His Meta article is useful for a different wiki thread: how AI strategy, performance metrics, and reorg pressure can damage engineering culture when leadership treats engineers as training-data inputs rather than autonomous owners.

people/gomer-bolstrood.title: gomer-bolstrood
people/gomer-bolstrood.content:
Gomer Bolstrood
Protestant political actor in Quicksilver, introduced into Eliza’s Book Two Hague network.
Role in Book Two
Gomer Bolstrood appears in the Hague material around English succession politics, anti-James maneuvering, and procurement. Eliza works with him around lead, saltpeter, and gunpowder, which makes him part of the book’s conversion of commodities into political force.
The source leaves an important ambiguity around Bolstrood’s unnamed Client. That uncertainty matters because it keeps the procurement thread from reducing to simple trade. Someone is using materials, markets, and Eliza’s intelligence to prepare political action.
Interpretation
Bolstrood’s Book Two role is to make the English succession crisis materially concrete. Rebellion is not only claims, speeches, or court alignments. It requires powder, lead, money, transport, plausible deniability, and people who can move among Dutch, English, and French networks.

people/goto-dengo.title: goto-dengo
people/goto-dengo.content:
Goto Dengo
Goto Dengo is a Japanese engineer in Cryptonomicon. He appears in both the WWII and modern timelines and becomes one of the novel’s central figures for engineering, guilt, survival, and moral reconstruction.
WWII Timeline
Goto first appears in the Shanghai material as a large Japanese soldier who meets Bobby Shaftoe in a sushi restaurant. The encounter begins as a brawl but becomes an unlikely friendship. Goto teaches Shaftoe judo; Shaftoe teaches Goto how to throw a baseball or grenade with better form. Their friendship is cut off by the approach of war.
In the Bismarck Sea sequence, Goto is aboard a Japanese troop transport when American planes attack with skip-bombing. The event breaks his confidence in the Japanese warrior worldview. He sees that the Americans have changed tactics, accepted a new technique, and applied it successfully. From inside his military culture, this seems dishonorably flexible; from the novel’s broader frame, it is a decisive advantage.
Goto survives by acting against the warrior code around him. He reaches for life preservers instead of standing in place to die honorably. The narration explicitly marks this as the moment when he has already lost the war inwardly.
Later in the war, Goto is forced into the Golgotha project in the Philippines: an underground vault built to hide Nazi and Japanese war gold. He engineers the site, but it is also a mass grave and death-trap. This leaves him with a burden that survives the end of the war.
After the War
After Manila, Goto has confessed to a priest and is approached by Enoch Root. Root wants him to help decide what should happen to the buried gold.
Goto resists the idea that gold itself can heal the world. He argues that stored gold is dead wealth, while true wealth comes from work, education, and building. Root answers that gold can still become books, bandages, schools, and hospitals. Goto’s condition is that if the gold comes out of the ground, it should be used so that wars like this do not happen again.
In the modern timeline, Goto appears as an old man and head of Goto Engineering. He knows the secret of Golgotha and eventually becomes part of the effort to recover and redirect the gold.
Worldview Shift
He begins inside an imperial Japanese warrior culture that values honor, spiritual invulnerability, and death over adaptive survival. The Bismarck Sea chapter shows him recognizing the power of an American style of tactical and technological flexibility. Later, his engineering skill is used in atrocity, which shows that technical competence by itself is not morally redeeming.
Goto’s final importance is that he has to convert engineering, guilt, and buried wealth into responsibility. He is neither a simple enemy nor a simple convert to the Allied worldview. He is a witness to what happens when technical intelligence serves empire, and then a participant in trying to turn blood money into repair.
Backlinks

Cryptonomicon Dual Timeline

people/gottfried-wilhelm-leibniz.title: gottfried-wilhelm-leibniz
people/gottfried-wilhelm-leibniz.content:
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
Historical philosopher, mathematician, jurist, librarian, and court adviser fictionalized in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver. This page is a focused person note, with fuller plot coverage in Quicksilver Plot.
Role in Quicksilver
In Book One, Leibniz is central before he is fully present. Daniel Waterhouse’s Logic Mill is explicitly Leibnizian in inspiration, Princess Caroline of Ansbach is friendly to Leibniz, and the 1713 mission to recall Daniel is driven by the Newton-Leibniz calculus priority dispute.
Book Two brings Leibniz directly into Jack and Eliza’s strand. He appears at Leipzig as “the Doctor,” trying to sell Kuxen in Brunswick-Luneburg silver mines while also thinking about binary arithmetic, cryptography, universal knowledge, matter, machines, and patronage.
Leipzig and Calculus
In Leipzig in 1684, Leibniz submits a mathematical manuscript to the Acta Eruditorum. The scene presents him as knowing that the method is powerful, that only he and Newton understand it, and that Newton may have invented an earlier version without publishing it.
This publication moment is important for the later priority dispute. It shows Leibniz deciding not to wait indefinitely for Newton, while also making clear that priority, publication, secrecy, and reputation are already entangled.
Mines, Quicksilver, and Enoch
Leibniz is responsible for improving or reviving silver mines in the Harz. He uses wind-powered machinery to drain mines and seeks investors who might accept a technically sophisticated but commercially difficult project.
Enoch Root works near this mining system as an alchemical and practical expert. Enoch’s lesson to Jack shows that quicksilver can extract silver from low-grade ore and that access to mercury gives Leibniz leverage over mine-masters.
Eliza and Cipher
Leibniz teaches Eliza a binary-based cipher. This connects Eliza’s practical intrigue to the same symbolic project that links Wilkins’s cryptography, Leibniz’s binary arithmetic, Daniel’s Logic Mill, and the later Newton-Leibniz dispute.
In Book Three, Leibniz’s correspondence with Eliza becomes a channel for cipher practice, genealogy, dynastic strategy, natural philosophy, and emotional confidence. From Venice, he writes about William and Mary, Peter of Russia, English war finance, Princess Eleanor, young Caroline, and his dissatisfaction with Newton’s account of force.

people/iain-m-banks.title: iain-m-banks
people/iain-m-banks.content:
Iain M. Banks
Scottish author (1954–2013). Published mainstream literary fiction as “Iain Banks” and science fiction as “Iain M. Banks.” The Culture series is his defining SF work.
Culture Series
A sequence of loosely connected novels set in the same fictional universe, centring on the Culture — a post-scarcity, machine-governed interstellar civilization. The novels vary widely in tone and focus; they can be read independently.

Consider Phlebas (1987) — first Culture novel; see Consider Phlebas
The Player of Games (1988) — second Culture novel; see The Player of Games

Notes
The Culture series is explicitly political: Banks was a democratic socialist, and the Culture is a thought experiment in what a truly post-scarcity, non-hierarchical, hedonistic society might look like. The books frequently examine what happens at the edges of that civilization, or from outside it.
Backlinks

Consider Phlebas
The Player of Games
The Culture

people/jack-shaftoe.title: jack-shaftoe
people/jack-shaftoe.content:
Jack Shaftoe
Fictional vagabond, soldier, performer, courier, and opportunist in Neal Stephenson’s Quicksilver. This page focuses on his Book Two arc; fuller Volume One chronology is in Quicksilver Plot.
Names and Frame
Jack is called Half-Cocked Jack and, in French circles, l’Emmerdeur. Book Two is titled King of the Vagabonds, though this note does not treat that title as a stable formal office.
East London Origin
Jack grows up on the Isle of Dogs with his half-brothers Dick and Bob. The boys begin as mudlarks and thieves around the Thames. Dick dies during a botched anchor theft from a Dutch vessel, a death that leaves Jack and Bob with guilt and nightmares.
After John Cole is sentenced to hang, Jack and Bob agree to hang from Cole’s legs to hasten his death. They then turn that skill into a trade among Newgate’s condemned prisoners, using theatrical performance to advertise the value of paying them for a faster hanging.
Continental Career
By 1683, Jack has moved through fake soldiering, plague-house squatting, smuggling, theft, and camp-following on the Continent. He joins the Christian relief of Vienna as a musketeer less from piety than from opportunity.
After the Ottoman camp collapses, Jack captures the Turkish warhorse Turk, pursues an ostrich for its plumes, and follows it into a Turkish mine under Vienna. There he interrupts the execution of harem women and rescues Eliza, setting up the main Book Two partnership.
Skills
Jack is illiterate, but he reads cities, roads, gallows, prisons, soldiers, crowds, horses, and criminal networks with unusual speed. He speaks enough French, English, zargon, and street code to move among vagabonds, soldiers, brokers, rat-catchers, and bankers’ messengers.
His strongest practical tools are mobility, performance, observation, and improvisational violence. His weakest point is deliberation: the narration repeatedly presents his body, appetites, fear, guilt, and pride as acting before his judgment catches up.
Relationship with Eliza
Jack saves Eliza, but she rapidly becomes more than a rescued dependent. She pushes him away from quick thieves’ markets and toward luxury trade, Leipzig, Amsterdam, banking contacts, and long-range plans.
Their partnership is unequal in visible power but increasingly reciprocal in function: Jack can cross borders, fight, ride, and vanish; Eliza can negotiate, perform gentility, read markets, and convert loot into leverage.
Paris Hallucination
After repeated courier rides between Paris and Lyons, Jack returns toward Paris exhausted and destabilized. The Paris hallucination sequence makes his guilt, illness, sexual shame, fear of predestination, fear of Catholic confession, and quicksilver imagery into a grotesque street pageant.
The scene does not resolve whether the visions are caused by syphilis, exhaustion, trauma, cold, mercury imagery, religious panic, or narrative style. The safest current reading is that the episode deliberately leaves several causes active at once.
After Paris, Jack is caught in the machinery of French noble power and galley punishment. John Churchill’s intervention saves him from immediate death but does not restore his freedom.
Book Two Ending
Jack returns to Amsterdam with Yevgeny and plunder, but cannot settle into the kind of respectable household identity he imagines with Eliza. Mr. Vliet and Mr. Foot convert his risk into maritime finance aboard God’s Wounds.
The voyage ends off the coast of Europe and North Africa when Barbary corsairs capture the ship. Jack tries to turn the fight into heroic reputation, but the crew cannot understand him. Book Two closes with him chained to a galley oar, reversing his earlier rescue of Eliza from slavery and execution under Vienna.

people/james-cowling.title: james-cowling
people/james-cowling.content:
James Cowling
James Cowling is presented in the May 25, 2026 Peterman Pod episode as CTO at Convex and a former senior principal engineer at Dropbox.
In This Wiki

Peterman Pod: James Cowling on Dropbox Systems and AI-Era Engineering

Source-Backed Notes
Cowling’s episode connects several technical threads: distributed transactions from his PhD work, Viewstamped Replication Revisited, Dropbox’s Magic Pocket storage system, multi-region storage, erasure coding, large-scale migration, and Convex’s developer-platform abstractions.
His strongest engineering-management idea is that teams should orient around the problem they solve rather than the system they own. In the Dropbox example, the Magic Pocket team became the Storage team so the team would be responsible for storage needs rather than defending one implementation.
His AI-era career advice emphasizes continuing to practice judgment and problem solving. He argues that coding agents do not remove the need for architecture, design, understanding, and real feedback from difficult work.
His episode is the main anchor for Choosing What to Work On, especially the idea that engineers should choose problems that matter rather than defend a current system or optimize for work that merely looks sophisticated.

people/james-everingham.title: james-everingham
people/james-everingham.content:
James Everingham
James Everingham is a software engineering leader best known as the former Head of Engineering at Instagram. He has a long career in Silicon Valley dating back to pre-IPO Netscape in 1995. He is currently CEO of Guild.ai, building AI agent governance infrastructure.
Career













































PeriodCompany / RoleTeen yearsSelf-taught; wrote shareware, phone phreaking software~1994Borland (developer tools; recruited based on reputation)1995–~2000Netscape (pre-IPO engineer; browser development)Post-NetscapeTell Me Networks~2010sLuminate (founder; Instagram-shopping concept; sold to Yahoo)~2013–~2018Instagram, Head of Engineering (scaled team to 100+ engineers)~2018–~2022Meta/Novi (led Libra/cryptocurrency project under David Marcus)~2022–~2023Lightspark (co-founder with David Marcus)2023–Guild.ai (CEO; AI agent control plane)
He entered the industry unconventionally: kicked out of Penn State at 18 for skipping classes to program, he was hired as a full-time computer scientist — reportedly the first non-degreed hire in 50+ years at that employer. His teen years included phone phreaking that brought an FBI visit to his home.
Key Ideas

Small teams with clear ownership beat large teams with dependencies. Instagram Stories (3 months, small team) and Boomerang (intern hackathon, 3 days) are his canonical examples.
Distribution beats technology in market competition. Lesson from watching Netscape lose to IE despite superior product: Microsoft won via Windows bundling, not browser quality.
CTO vs. VP Eng are distinct roles: CTO owns roadmap, new tech, and external brand; VP Eng owns hiring, execution, org structure, and performance.
Mentorship is earned through usefulness: Don’t ask for a mentor directly; offer to help instead.
Deterministic governance layers are needed for AI agents: Guild.ai’s thesis is that enterprises need controlled, auditable infrastructure around non-deterministic LLM systems.

Career Regrets (self-reported)

Not treating time as the true scarce resource earlier.
Staying in Pennsylvania 7 years instead of moving to Silicon Valley sooner.
Not maxing his 401(k) from day one.
Being too quiet early in career out of fear of looking foolish.
Not being deliberate enough about relationship building (“friends come and go, but enemies collect”).

Sources

Peterman Pod: Ex-Head of Eng at Instagram — April 6, 2026

Backlinks

The Peterman Pod
Ryan Peterman

people/jean-antoine-de-mesmes-d-avaux.title: jean-antoine-de-mesmes-d-avaux
people/jean-antoine-de-mesmes-d-avaux.content:
Jean Antoine de Mesmes, comte d’Avaux
French ambassador in the Dutch Republic, fictionalized in Quicksilver. This page focuses on his Book Two role.
Role in Book Two
D’Avaux introduces Eliza into the Hague’s diplomatic and succession world. Through him she meets Mary of Orange, Monmouth, Gomer Bolstrood, and other actors whose conversations connect English succession, French pressure, Dutch interests, procurement, and market speculation.
He is useful to Eliza because he opens rooms. He is dangerous because those rooms belong to the French state and to noble power. His manners and access are part of the machinery.
By the end of Book Two, d’Avaux is escorting Eliza out of Amsterdam toward France. William of Orange’s interception makes explicit what was already true: Eliza has become valuable enough that French and Dutch strategy both want to route her.
Interpretation
D’Avaux represents diplomacy as an information market. He does not only exchange messages between states. He identifies useful people, stages encounters, manages impressions, and turns social access into political leverage.

people/john-churchill.title: john-churchill
people/john-churchill.content:
John Churchill
Historical soldier and court figure fictionalized in Quicksilver. This page focuses on his Book Two role.
Role in Book Two
John Churchill sits between Jack Shaftoe’s low-world soldiering and English succession politics. Jack’s winter narration links Churchill to Bob Shaftoe, Monmouth, Maestricht, and a version of soldierly honor that Jack both envies and distrusts.
Churchill’s most important Book Two intervention comes in Paris. Jack’s exhaustion, failed horse-market plan, and public violence put him near death. Churchill recognizes him during a military and courtly spectacle, prevents an immediate killing, and converts the situation into a narrower punishment path.
That rescue is not freedom. Churchill saves Jack from being killed on the spot, but Jack remains inside coercive French power and is still pushed toward galley punishment.
Interpretation
Churchill’s function in Book Two is not simple benevolence. He can recognize Jack as a useful, known, and embarrassing survivor from earlier wars, but he acts within court, army, and succession systems. His intervention shows how elite military intelligence can redirect violence without abolishing it.

people/john-myles-white.title: john-myles-white
people/john-myles-white.content:
John Myles White
John Myles White is presented in the Peterman Pod episode as a former Meta Superintelligence Labs engineering director, a former PyTorch engineering leader, a Julia core contributor, and a statistics-oriented engineer.
In This Wiki
White’s episode is useful for three wiki threads:

Big-Tech Promotion Culture: promotion incentives can distort system design and team health.
Engineering Career Growth: team choice and local incentive systems shape what kind of engineer someone becomes.
Choosing What to Work On: promotion-first work can crowd out durable engineering judgment.
Statistics Book Recommendations: White gives unusually strong recommendations for Larry Wasserman and Peter Aronow.

Notes
White treats the promotion game as real rather than imaginary. His practical recommendation is not naive refusal; it is to choose teams and managers whose incentive systems make good engineering more likely.
He also links statistical rigor to engineering judgment. In his A/B testing stories, weak understanding of statistical significance combines with organizational goals to produce decisions that are mathematically and operationally suspect.
Backlinks

The Peterman Pod

people/larry-wasserman.title: larry-wasserman
people/larry-wasserman.content:
Larry Wasserman
Larry Wasserman is the author of All of Statistics, a concise course in probability, statistical inference, and modern statistical learning.
In This Wiki
Wasserman enters the wiki through two connected sources:

John Myles White’s Peterman Pod episode, where White strongly recommends Wasserman’s statistics books for their honesty about assumptions and limits.
The user’s active rereading of All of Statistics, currently ingested through Chapter 1.

Reading Significance
The preface to All of Statistics presents the book as a fast route for mathematically prepared readers who need probability and statistics for data analysis, machine learning, and statistical inference. Wasserman explicitly connects statistics, data mining, and machine learning as overlapping practices for collecting and analyzing data.
For this wiki, Wasserman is useful as a bridge between software-engineering empirical practice and formal statistical reasoning.

All of Statistics Reading

people/marc-brooker.title: marc-brooker
people/marc-brooker.content:
Marc Brooker
Marc Brooker is presented in the Peterman Pod episode as an AWS distinguished engineer who has worked across cloud systems, databases, serverless infrastructure, and production reliability.
In This Wiki
Brooker’s episode is useful for three wiki threads:

Postmortem Culture: incidents and on-call work are treated as a primary source of distributed-systems learning.
Engineering Career Growth: senior engineers need both hands-on practice and enough communication to find important problems.
Choosing What to Work On: customer pain, technical change, and repeated incident patterns are treated as problem-selection signals.
Database Systems: the episode connects serverless workloads, relational data, block/object storage trends, and cache consistency to database architecture.

Notes
Brooker frames practical expertise as groundedness in real systems. Fundamentals matter, but production failures, customer behavior, and postmortems reveal constraints that do not appear in classroom examples.
His doing-versus-discussing model is also a career-warning pattern. Pure practitioners can become under-leveraged; pure communicators can become detached from reality.

people/martin-kleppmann.title: martin-kleppmann
people/martin-kleppmann.content:
Martin Kleppmann
Martin Kleppmann is presented in the Pragmatic Engineer episode as the author of Designing Data-Intensive Applications and as a distributed-systems researcher at the University of Cambridge.
In This Wiki
Kleppmann’s episode is useful for three wiki threads:

Database Systems: the book explains replication, partitioning, consistency, transactions, and processing patterns as durable architecture tradeoffs.
Distributed Systems: the interview emphasizes failure, concurrency, delays, and the limits of informal reasoning.
Local-First Software: Kleppmann connects user-owned local state with replication and collaboration techniques.

Notes
The episode presents Kleppmann as a translator between practice and research. His industry background helps explain why Designing Data-Intensive Applications became useful to working engineers, while his academic work gives the discussion a sharper focus on correctness, formal reasoning, and collaboration protocols.

Michael Bolin
Michael Bolin is presented in the March 9, 2026 Peterman Pod episode as the tech lead for the open source Codex repository and a former distinguished engineer at Meta.

Source-Backed Notes
Bolin’s episode connects several phases of developer-tooling work: Chickenfoot for end-user browser automation, Google web products and JavaScript tooling, Facebook/Meta build and IDE tooling, virtual file systems, and OpenAI Codex.
His career advice emphasizes alignment between technical passion and company priorities, scoped project starts, technical depth, and writing as a planning and communication tool.

people/mike-stonebraker.title: mike-stonebraker
people/mike-stonebraker.content:
Mike Stonebraker
Mike Stonebraker is a computer scientist and Turing Award winner. In the Peterman Pod episode, he discusses the origins of Ingres and Postgres, his critique of one-size-fits-all database systems, his disagreements with Google and Amazon database choices, and the DBOS approach to durable workflow state.
Key Ideas From The Episode
Stonebraker says he entered databases at Berkeley after Gene Wong pointed him toward the area soon after Ted Codd’s relational-model paper. He presents Ingres as both his tenure project and a serious academic system that became widely used on Unix before its commercial path began.
He frames Postgres as a response to Ingres’s type-system limits. Standard primitive types were not enough for workloads such as GIS or finance, so Postgres made extensibility central: users could add domain-specific types and operations that remained efficient inside the database.
Stonebraker’s “one size fits none” argument is that very different workloads need very different architectures. He still treats Postgres as a strong default for many ordinary applications, but says high-end warehouses, stream processing, vector processing, and high-throughput transactional workloads need more specialized systems.
DBOS and Current Work
Stonebraker describes DBOS as growing from the idea that much operating-system and workflow state is really database state. The commercial company focuses on durable, transactional workflows in languages such as TypeScript, Java, Go, and Python.
He expects read-write agentic AI applications to become database problems because they will need atomicity, consistency, durability, and coordinated commits across actions.
Career Notes
Stonebraker says he prefers academia and startups to big companies because large companies bring bosses, rules, publication constraints, and internal politics. His advice to PhD graduates is to take a prestigious job, find a mentor, and work on contrarian problems rather than simply following the current trend.
That contrarian-problem advice now supports Choosing What to Work On.
Backlinks

people/neal-stephenson.title: neal-stephenson
people/neal-stephenson.content:
Neal Stephenson
American speculative fiction author. Known for dense, idea-driven novels at the intersection of science, philosophy, history, and technology.
Works Mentioned in Sources

Cryptonomicon (1999) — dual-timeline novel (WWII codebreaking / 1990s internet/cryptography); see Cryptonomicon
Anathem (2008) — #1 NYT Bestseller; philosophical science fiction; see Anathem
Snow Crash — referenced in Cryptonomicon blurb copy
The Diamond Age — referenced in Cryptonomicon blurb copy
Quicksilver (2003) — Volume One of The Baroque Cycle; 17th/early-18th-century historical fiction about natural philosophy, alchemy, finance, computation, and the Newton-Leibniz dispute; see Quicksilver

Notes
Anathem acknowledges a deep philosophical lineage (Thales, Plato, Leibniz, Kant, Gödel, Husserl) and institutional inspiration from the Long Now Foundation. Stephenson invented a full constructed language (Orth) and neologism system for the book rather than use real-world terminology, to preserve thematic resonance for Earth readers.
Cryptonomicon collaborated with real cryptographers: Bruce Schneier invented the Solitaire cipher for the novel (card-deck based; functional) and wrote an appendix explaining it. Ian Goldberg contributed Perl code that appears in the text as a character’s email. Stephenson’s acknowledgments use WWII as a “gratitude cutoff date” — only thanks people who contributed after the war ends.
Quicksilver connects Stephenson’s recurring interest in cryptography and computation to early modern natural philosophy: John Wilkins’s fictionalized Cryptonomicon, Wilkins’s Universal Character, Daniel Waterhouse’s Logic Mill, Newton’s fluxions, Leibniz’s binary ideas, and the Royal Society all appear in the ingested portion. Book Two adds a second register: Jack Shaftoe and Eliza move through war, slavery, vagabondage, commerce, mining, diplomacy, and market information.
Enoch Root is a cross-book connective figure: in the ingested portion of Quicksilver he is an alchemist/natural philosopher carrying Princess Caroline’s message to Daniel Waterhouse; in Cryptonomicon he appears in both the WWII and modern plots as a religious, medical, cryptographic, and operational intermediary.
Cryptonomicon contains an explicit Athena/Ares framework, delivered by Enoch Root, that treats technology and metis as civilization’s defense against recurring violent disorder. The Quicksilver ship Minerva may connect to this same symbolic field, though that cross-book link is currently interpretive rather than directly stated in the source.
Backlinks

people/simon-johnson.title: simon-johnson
people/simon-johnson.content:
Simon Johnson
Simon Johnson is presented in the Power and Progress ebook metadata as the Ronald A. Kurtz Professor of Entrepreneurship at MIT Sloan, head of the Global Economics and Management group, and a former chief economist at the International Monetary Fund.
In This Wiki
Johnson’s coauthored argument in Power and Progress gives the wiki a source-backed bridge between economic crises, institutional design, technology policy, and labor power.
The book is most useful here for its claim that AI policy should not be limited to technical safety or innovation speed. It should also ask whether digital tools create useful human tasks, strengthen democratic voice, or instead concentrate power through automation, surveillance, and targeted advertising.

people/steve-yegge.title: steve-yegge
people/steve-yegge.content:
Steve Yegge
Steve Yegge is the author of The Last Technical Interview. The Medium article page describes him as formerly at Geoworks, Amazon, Google, Grab, and Sourcegraph, with long experience in software engineering and technical interviewing.
Ideas In This Wiki
Yegge’s current contribution to this wiki is a critique of technical interviews as weak hiring signal. He argues that the standard interview loop is an artificial work simulation and that the replacement should move toward real paid work with the team.
His proposed Campfire Hiring model is useful because it does not stop at work samples. It also asks what the candidate keeps after the process: a portable record of the work and the evaluator’s assessment.

people/tom-di-mino.title: tom-di-mino
people/tom-di-mino.content:
Tom Di Mino
Tom Di Mino is described by AI Clambake as a self-taught AI engineer and amateur linguist who claims to have deciphered Linear A. The same article says his claim is under review by linguistics experts at Rutgers and Cambridge.
The source reports that Di Mino has studied classical history, linguistics, and languages for years, including Attic Greek, classical Latin, Sanskrit, Arabic, and Ugaritic. It also says he had studied Linear A for 7 years before beginning the current decipherment work in January 2026.
Wiki Relevance

Claimed a Semitic-language decipherment of Linear A.
Reportedly used Claude Code to build Python scripts for corpus querying, cross-reference, and organization.
Serves as the human domain-expertise side of AI-Assisted Decipherment, assuming the reported workflow is accurate.

Caveat
This page should not be read as saying Di Mino has conclusively deciphered Linear A. The only ingested source is an early AI Clambake report. The result still needs independent expert validation and publication of the evidence trail.
Backlinks
