projects/peterman-pod-james-everingham-i.title: peterman-pod-james-everingham-instagram-career-regrets
projects/peterman-pod-james-everingham-i.content:
Peterman Pod: Ex-Head of Eng at Instagram — Career Regrets and Learnings
This April 6, 2026 episode of The Peterman Pod features James Everingham, former Head of Engineering at Instagram, discussing his unconventional career arc from Netscape to Instagram to AI agent infrastructure at Guild.ai.
Core Claims
The episode’s central argument is that the most valuable engineering leadership insights come from constraints: small teams move faster than large ones, distribution defeats technology, and career regrets are almost always about personal habits (speaking up, relocating, saving) rather than technical choices.
Engineering Leadership Principles
Small teams win
Everingham’s strongest recurring theme: capable, small teams with clear ownership and minimal dependencies consistently outperform larger teams. He cites Instagram Stories (3 months, small team) and Boomerang (intern Tim Leonardo, 3-day hackathon) as proof. His prescription: identify the dependencies slowing a large team and eliminate them, or restructure into smaller units.
Do the simple thing first
Prove the thesis on a small scale before scaling headcount or scope.
Remove blockers, ask questions, don’t prescribe
His management style is to hire people smarter than himself, clear obstacles, and ask questions rather than dictate solutions. He uses close oversight only temporarily when a team is underperforming, then steps back.
The CTO vs. VP Engineering Distinction

























CTOVP EngineeringTechnology roadmapHiring and headcountEvaluating new technologiesExecution and deliveryProduct visionOrg structureExternal brand and talksPerformance management
This is a clean separation Everingham experienced directly at Instagram alongside Mike Krieger (CTO).
Distribution Beats Technology
Watching Netscape lose to IE is the formative lesson. Microsoft bundled IE with Windows; Netscape had a superior browser. The market outcome was determined by distribution, not quality. This shaped his view that product decisions should account for how software reaches users, not just what it does.
On Networking

“If you want a mentor, don’t ask someone ‘I need a mentor.’ Go offer them help.”

Build relationships through usefulness and genuine curiosity. Many of his Netscape colleagues (including Hadi Partovi, who was on the IE team) later became allies and collaborators.
Career Regrets
Everingham is unusually candid about regrets. All five are personal/structural rather than technical:

Not treating time as scarce: The most important resource shifts from money (early career) to time (later career). Act accordingly sooner.
Delayed relocation: Seven years in Pennsylvania before moving to Silicon Valley. The network and mentorship density of a hub matters.
Not maximizing 401(k) from day one: Compound interest is significant over decades; deferring costs real money.
Being too quiet: Held back from speaking up out of fear of looking foolish. Successful people speak despite uncertainty.
Relationship debt: “Friends come and go, but enemies collect.” Don’t burn bridges; you will encounter people again.

Guild.ai Thesis
Everingham’s current company builds a “deterministic layer on top of non-deterministic technology” — infrastructure for governing AI agents in enterprise: access control, audit logs, oversight policies. The analogy is employee permission systems: just as a company controls what a human employee can access, it needs equivalent controls for autonomous AI agents.
Notable Stories

FBI visit as a teenager: His phone phreaking software was traced back to him; FBI showed up at his house. He was not charged. This was a formative brush with consequence from building software without understanding its reach.
Netscape open-source: When open-sourcing the Netscape browser, SSL code was classified as a munition and couldn’t be exported. The team had to manually censor profanity in the codebase; they couldn’t grep for unknown offensive terms.
IE “E” prank: Microsoft placed a giant “E” logo on Netscape’s front lawn. Everingham later worked with Hadi Partovi (the IE lead) at a different company.
Boomerang: Intern Tim Leonardo built the Boomerang feature in a 3-day hackathon; it became one of Instagram’s most-used features.

Backlinks

The Peterman Pod
James Everingham
Ryan Peterman

projects/peterman-pod-john-myles-white-p.title: peterman-pod-john-myles-white-promo-hacking
projects/peterman-pod-john-myles-white-p.content:
Peterman Pod: John Myles White on Promo Hacking and Industry Shifts
This May 4, 2026 episode of The Peterman Pod features John Myles White, a former Meta/MSL engineering director, PyTorch leader, Julia core contributor, and statistics-oriented engineer.
Core Claims
White argues that promotion culture can become strong enough to damage engineering quality. In his Meta examples, engineers sometimes chose broad, coupled, or short-lived projects because those projects created promotion narratives, even when the work was not technically sound or durable.
The episode does not present “ignore the game” as a serious option inside a promotion-driven org. White says local incentives matter, and that the more practical move is choosing teams and leaders whose culture rewards engineering craft rather than pure promotion velocity.
Major Themes

Employee leverage: White says the post-layoff big-tech labor market gives many employees less leverage than in the pre-COVID period.
Promotion distortion: promotion bars can reward scope theater, unnecessary coupling, and work that teams plan to delete after the review cycle.
PyTorch as counterexample: PyTorch selected for craft-oriented engineers, accepted slower promotion velocity, and gained credibility from a high bar.
Choosing teams and work: White’s examples are an anti-signal for Choosing What to Work On: work chosen mainly for promotion optics can undermine durable engineering value.
Experimentation rigor: White connects weak statistical understanding to bad A/B-test decisions and goal-driven shipping.
Language performance: Julia appealed to White because it aimed to make high-level scientific programming fast without hiding all performance in low-level libraries.
Career regret: White regrets not using skip-level office hours and recommends that junior engineers bring useful ground truth to leaders.

Reading Recommendations
White’s statistics recommendations are tracked in Statistics Book Recommendations. The most source-backed titles are All of Statistics by Larry Wasserman and Foundations of Agnostic Statistics by Peter M. Aronow and Benjamin T. Miller.

projects/peterman-pod-marc-brooker-aws-i.title: peterman-pod-marc-brooker-aws-incidents
projects/peterman-pod-marc-brooker-aws-i.content:
Peterman Pod: Marc Brooker on AWS Incidents and Engineering Change
This April 13, 2026 episode of The Peterman Pod features Marc Brooker, an AWS distinguished engineer, discussing production incidents, distributed-systems learning, AI-assisted engineering, writing, and career judgment.
Core Claims
Brooker presents production work as the missing layer between computer-science fundamentals and real distributed-systems expertise. On-call rotations, customer behavior, and postmortems expose how systems actually fail and where repeated failure patterns suggest deeper architectural or organizational work.
He also argues that AI increases the need for hands-on senior technical leadership. Senior engineers who stay too far from the tools can become detached from the new practical constraints, while engineers who only practice and never communicate may miss higher-leverage problems.
Major Themes

Problem discovery: important work comes from both customer pain and technical changes such as faster networks, storage, GPUs, and changing data backends.
Postmortem quality: good postmortems explain what happened, why it happened at multiple levels, and what recurring pattern the incident reveals.
Caching risk: caches add state, invalidation, and consistency complexity rather than being a free performance layer.
AI tooling: direct use of AI tools is necessary for both junior and senior engineers to understand the new development loop.
Doing versus discussing: Brooker aims for roughly 75/25 or 80/20 practitioner versus teaching and communication.
Career boldness: Brooker says he might have changed organizations slightly more often when learning and impact tapered.

projects/peterman-pod-michael-bolin-code.title: peterman-pod-michael-bolin-codex-career
projects/peterman-pod-michael-bolin-code.content:
Peterman Pod: Michael Bolin on Codex and Developer Tools
This March 9, 2026 episode of The Peterman Pod features Michael Bolin, the OpenAI Codex tech lead and a former Meta distinguished engineer.
Core Claims
Bolin’s career story is a developer-tools arc: end-user browser automation, Google web products, Facebook build and IDE tooling, Meta virtual file systems, and then OpenAI Codex.
The episode’s main engineering lesson is that tool builders need both technical depth and organizational alignment. Bolin repeatedly found leverage where a developer workflow was painfully slow, then made a concrete, scoped improvement that could earn adoption.
Major Themes

Developer-tooling leverage: Buck and Nucleide grew out of direct pain in mobile development workflows.
Scoped influence: Bolin describes starting with a narrower problem before broader adoption.
Choosing technical work: workflow pain, concrete scope, and company alignment make the Buck story a useful example for Choosing What to Work On.
Agent context: Codex benefits from repo instructions, READMEs, MCP tool definitions, and search tools such as ripgrep.
Research-led culture: OpenAI required adjustment from engineering-led big-tech cultures, but the closeness to model builders made Codex work more valuable.
Technical depth: lower-level operating-systems knowledge can reveal performance and architecture options that abstractions hide.

projects/peterman-pod-mike-stonebraker-p.title: peterman-pod-mike-stonebraker-postgres
projects/peterman-pod-mike-stonebraker-p.content:
Peterman Pod: Turing Award Winner Mike Stonebraker
This April 20, 2026 episode of The Peterman Pod features Mike Stonebraker, a Turing Award winner known for foundational work on database systems, Ingres, Postgres, and later database-backed workflow systems such as DBOS.
Core Claims
Stonebraker presents database history as a series of architecture-fit problems. Relational databases improved on pointer-following and hierarchical systems because they separated logical queries from physical storage more cleanly. Postgres extended that pattern by making type systems extensible, so the database could support domain-specific data rather than forcing every workload into a fixed set of primitive types.
The episode also argues against database monoculture at the high end. Stonebraker says Postgres is an excellent low-end default, but large data warehouses, stream processing, vector search, and high-throughput transaction systems often need specialized architectures.
Major Themes

Relational databases: Stonebraker frames Codd’s relational model as the right response to older physical-data-model systems.
Postgres extensibility: Postgres was designed to support custom data types and operations for domains such as GIS and finance.
One size fits none: specialized database architectures can outperform general-purpose systems by large margins on matched workloads.
Consistency and integrity: Stonebraker criticizes eventual consistency when applications need hard integrity constraints.
DBOS: database-backed state management can make workflows durable, transactional, and easier to recover after failures.
Text-to-SQL realism: production data warehouses have messy schemas, duplicated data, and complex queries that current benchmark results can obscure.
Research career: Stonebraker recommends mentors, contrarian problems, and work that does not simply follow the current trend.

Backlinks

projects/policy-on-the-ai-exponential.title: policy-on-the-ai-exponential
projects/policy-on-the-ai-exponential.content:
Policy on the AI Exponential
Policy on the AI Exponential is a June 2026 essay by Dario Amodei about how policy should respond if AI capability growth continues to compound faster than ordinary institutions can react.
The essay is the initial anchor for this wiki’s Post-AI World section.
Core Argument
Amodei argues that AI has crossed from a mostly speculative policy concern into a strategically consequential technology. His diagnosis is not only that AI is powerful. It is that the gap between AI’s rate of progress and policy’s rate of response has become dangerous.
Earlier measures such as transparency requirements, chip export controls, and labor-impact data collection preserved optionality while risks were harder to specify. The essay says that posture is no longer enough. Frontier AI now requires concrete rules for public safety, labor displacement, scientific acceleration, civil liberties, and geopolitical coordination.
Five Policy Domains
The essay covers five domains.
First, frontier model regulation should move beyond transparency. Models above a compute threshold should face mandatory third-party testing for cybersecurity, biological weapons, loss of control, and automated R&D risks. Release should be blockable or reversible when testing finds unacceptable risk, with safeguards against arbitrary or politicized action.
Second, macroeconomic policy should prepare for the possibility that AI creates rapid growth and enduring labor displacement at the same time. The near-term response is better measurement, retention incentives, wage insurance, training, and matching. The longer-term response may require income support, taxation of AI-created gains, or universal capital accounts.
Third, downstream science policy should not let old regulatory timelines swallow AI’s benefits. In biomedicine, agencies may need standards for AI simulation, toxicology prediction, dose selection, biomarkers, synthetic control arms, surrogate endpoints, and more flexible accelerated approval.
Fourth, civil-liberties policy needs to anticipate AI-enabled concentrations of power. Amodei’s examples include autonomous weapons, population-scale surveillance, bulk data-broker loopholes, and unequal access to AI during government action.
Fifth, geopolitics should be organized around a democratic AI coalition that coordinates supply-chain access, risk standards, benefit sharing, mutual defense, anti-repression norms, and macroeconomic stabilization.
Relationship To The Wiki
The essay is useful because it turns several existing wiki threads into one institutional agenda.
Recursive Self-Improvement explains why the pace of AI progress could become hard for human institutions to track. Amodei’s essay asks what governments should do if that pace is already visible.
Technology and Worker Power and Machine Usefulness ask whether technology becomes shared prosperity or concentrated control. Amodei adds a lab-leader policy version of that question: if AI creates hypergrowth and labor displacement, who receives the gains, and how does society preserve purpose and agency?
AI Commissioning and Agentic Self-Service Analytics show smaller-scale versions of the same shift. More work can be delegated to AI systems, but the human bottlenecks become review, governance, meaning, and source-of-truth maintenance.
Caveats
This is an agenda-setting essay by an AI lab CEO, not an independent empirical assessment. Its claims about frontier capability, labor displacement, biosecurity risk, model testing, and chip policy should be strengthened with companion policy documents and independent sources before the wiki treats them as settled.
The essay is still valuable because it is explicit about tradeoffs: regulation can miss the real risks, labor policy must address both money and meaning, downstream scientific regulation must accelerate without becoming unsafe, and AI power must be checked in both governments and companies.

projects/pragmatic-engineer-designing-da.title: pragmatic-engineer-designing-data-intensive-applications
projects/pragmatic-engineer-designing-da.content:
Pragmatic Engineer: Designing Data-intensive Applications with Martin Kleppmann
This April 22, 2026 episode of The Pragmatic Engineer features Martin Kleppmann, author of Designing Data-Intensive Applications, in conversation with Gergely Orosz.
Core Claims
The episode frames Designing Data-Intensive Applications as durable because it teaches recurring data-system tradeoffs rather than one product stack. Replication, partitioning, transactions, consistency, stream processing, and batch processing remain useful concepts even as individual tools change.
Kleppmann’s second-edition discussion updates the same frame for newer system patterns: cloud services, scaling down as well as scaling up, MapReduce’s historical role, replication versus sharding, formal verification, and local-first collaboration software. The key continuity is that engineers still need precise models of state, failure, and communication.
Major Themes

Fundamentals over recipes: the book’s value comes from reusable mental models.
Research-to-practice translation: Kleppmann’s industry and academic background lets him connect production engineering with formal distributed-systems ideas.
Second-edition scope: the data landscape changed, but the book still needs to teach tradeoffs rather than tool fashion.
Distributed-systems difficulty: failures, concurrency, and delays make intuitive reasoning unreliable.
Formal reasoning: verification and formal methods can clarify subtle correctness claims.
Local-first software: user-owned local state can improve responsiveness, ownership, and offline behavior while still supporting collaboration.

projects/quicksilver-book-two-king-of-th.title: quicksilver-book-two-king-of-the-vagabonds
projects/quicksilver-book-two-king-of-th.content:
Quicksilver Book Two: King of the Vagabonds
Book Two of Quicksilver, King of the Vagabonds, shifts the novel from Daniel Waterhouse’s Royal Society and Atlantic frame to Jack Shaftoe and Eliza. Its world is made from mudlarks, gallows, soldiers, camp followers, harem captives, trade fairs, silver mines, VOC shares, couriers, coffee houses, ambassadors, and galley slaves.
The governing idea is conversion. Jack and Eliza repeatedly watch one kind of value become another: plumes into money, money into Kuxen, quicksilver into silver, letters into intelligence, rumors into market movement, bodies into labor, and costume into rank. Jack understands roads, violence, horses, and informal people. Eliza understands languages, prices, social performance, and political rooms. Together they make Book Two the novel’s strongest bridge between underworld mobility and high-level finance.
Body of the Book









































MovementSummaryLondon mud and gallowsJack’s childhood with Bob and Dick makes poverty, theft, execution, and performance his first education.Vienna and Eliza’s rescueJack’s opportunistic soldiering becomes a rescue when he finds Eliza under Vienna after the Ottoman defeat.BohemiaJack and Eliza survive through ravaged postwar country and turn vagabond intelligence into route-finding.LeipzigThe fair teaches Eliza that goods, coin, shares, credit, and information can be converted without passing through simple cash.Saxony and the HarzGottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, Enoch Root, mine machinery, quicksilver, and Walpurgisnacht connect natural philosophy to commercial extraction.Amsterdam and ParisEliza fits Amsterdam’s market system; Jack becomes a courier through Paris, Lyons, brokers, horse markets, Huguenot danger, and police attention.The Hague and FranceJean Antoine de Mesmes, comte d’Avaux, Mary of Orange, Monmouth, and Gomer Bolstrood bring Eliza into succession politics and war procurement.Book Two endingEliza is pulled toward Versailles-facing intelligence work, while Jack is captured off the coast of North Africa and chained to a galley oar.
Main Threads

Vagabondage as intelligence: Jack cannot read books, but he reads bodies, crowds, roads, punishments, soldiers, and criminal systems.
Eliza’s commercial intelligence: Eliza turns captivity education, languages, market observation, costume, and correspondence into agency.
Slavery and punishment: The book links East London hanging, Barbary captivity, Ottoman harem power, Huguenot galley punishment, and Jack’s final enslavement.
Quicksilver and liquidity: Mercury is both a practical mining input and a metaphor for volatile conversion across markets, bodies, letters, and rumors.
Cipher and intelligence: Leibniz’s binary cipher, couriers, coffee houses, diplomatic rooms, and market rumors make information tradable.
Diplomatic theater: Opera boxes, ambassadors, noble names, and staged conversations turn politics into performance.

projects/quicksilver.title: quicksilver
projects/quicksilver.content:
Quicksilver
Author: Neal Stephenson
Published: 2003
Series: Volume One of The Baroque Cycle
Ingest status: Complete for Volume One: Book One (Quicksilver), Book Two (King of the Vagabonds), and Book Three (Odalisque).
Reading progress: Book Two (King of the Vagabonds) finished on 2026-06-23. The private raw summary already covers Book Three, but the confirmed current reading boundary is the end of Book Two.
Focused Book Two page: Quicksilver Book Two: King of the Vagabonds.
Summary
Quicksilver begins in 1713, when Enoch Root arrives in Boston and recruits the elderly Daniel Waterhouse to return to England. Princess Caroline of Ansbach wants Daniel involved in the worsening Newton-Leibniz priority dispute. Daniel has spent his Massachusetts exile building a Leibniz-inspired Logic Mill, but he leaves his wife Faith and son Godfrey and boards Minerva for England.
Daniel’s Atlantic voyage repeatedly opens into memories of Restoration England. He recalls Cambridge with Isaac Newton, the plague years with Wilkins and Hooke, the Great Fire of London, the early Royal Society, Newton’s optics and gravity work, alchemy, court patronage, and the growing problem of how knowledge, money, and power circulate. Book One ends with Minerva escaping Edward Teach’s pirate flotilla off Cape Cod after Daniel, Dappa, and van Hoek turn calculation, seamanship, and improvised ammunition into survival.
Book Two shifts to Jack Shaftoe, whose childhood and vagabond life move through mudlarks, execution crowds, soldiering, plague houses, and the relief of Vienna. Jack rescues Eliza, a Qwghlmian-born former slave shaped by Barbary captivity, harem politics, languages, and market intelligence. Their partnership turns loot into liquidity: ostrich plumes, horses, Kuxen, quicksilver, Dutch shares, ciphers, couriers, coffee houses, and rumors become convertible forms of power.
Eliza learns faster than Jack that commerce is political. In Leipzig, Amsterdam, Paris, and the Hague, she moves from survival partner to correspondent, trader, cipher-user, and intelligence source. Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz teaches her cipher methods while pursuing mining schemes and the calculus problem. Jack’s courier routes and Paris hallucination show the same system from below: underworld brokers, Huguenot danger, police surveillance, military processions, and bodily exhaustion.
Book Two ends with both halves of the partnership under French pressure. Jack is saved from immediate death by John Churchill but remains trapped in punishment machinery moving toward the galleys, while Eliza leaves Amsterdam under French diplomatic escort and is violently separated from her party.
Book Three, Odalisque, completes the volume’s political arc. Daniel watches Charles II die, sees James II’s regime curdle into paranoia, and is eventually tortured by Jeffreys for revolutionary intelligence. Eliza becomes Countess de la Zeur and Duchess of Qwghlm, manages noble assets, helps stage the false “Fall of Batavia” market operation, tracks slavery and the Taunton schoolgirls, and becomes entangled with d’Avaux, Rossignol, d’Arcachon, William of Orange, Princess Eleanor, and young Caroline.
The 1688-1689 sections connect the scientific, financial, and dynastic systems directly to the Glorious Revolution. Daniel finds the fleeing James II in Sheerness and chooses not to rescue the old regime. Eliza’s encoded letters reveal that her publicly dead infant is alive and hidden under refugee cover. The volume ends in Bishopsgate and Bedlam, where Pepys, Hooke, and Daniel’s Royal Society friends force him toward dangerous bladder-stone surgery, leaving his survival and later Massachusetts exile as the bridge back to the 1713 frame.
Major Threads

Science as institution: Newton, Hooke, Wilkins, Boyle, Wren, Huygens, and the Royal Society show discovery as experiment, correspondence, patronage, rivalry, and performance.
Newton versus Leibniz: The priority dispute is not only mathematical. It is tied to national pride, court politics, correspondence networks, and Daniel’s loyalty to both men.
Liquidity: Quicksilver, coin, shares, Kuxen, commodities, letters, bodies, titles, and intelligence all become moveable forms of value.
Vagabondage as intelligence: Jack’s world of mudlarks, soldiers, smugglers, couriers, rat-catchers, exiles, and captives carries information outside formal institutions.
Slavery and punishment: Eliza’s Barbary past, Huguenot galley punishment, African slavery, and Abigail Frome / the Taunton schoolgirls connect commerce to coerced bodies.
Revolution as finance and information: William’s invasion depends on letters, market signals, religious toleration politics, English elites, Dutch finance, and French intelligence failures.
Minerva / metis: The ship Minerva and Aegis imagery fit the book’s interest in cunning, practical intelligence, and technical craft, though the source does not explicitly prove a cross-book Athena/Ares/Metis reading.

Key Characters

















































CharacterRoleDaniel WaterhouseNatural philosopher, Puritan heir, Royal Society participant, Massachusetts exile, and 1713 returneeEnoch RootLong-lived alchemist/natural philosopher and messenger between Daniel, Caroline, Leibniz, and later Stephenson threadsIsaac NewtonDaniel’s Cambridge roommate; optics, fluxions, gravity, alchemy, and later priority-dispute figureGottfried Wilhelm LeibnizMathematician, philosopher, mining reformer, cipher teacher, and Newton’s rivalJack ShaftoeVagabond, soldier, courier, thief, and rescuer of ElizaElizaFormer slave turned trader, cipher correspondent, intelligence source, Countess de la Zeur, and Duchess of QwghlmBob ShaftoeJack’s brother; soldierly counterpart tied to Churchill and Monmouth’s rebellionJohn ChurchillSoldier and court figure whose interventions affect Jack, Bob, and English politicsWilliam of OrangeDutch prince and later English co-sovereign; organizer of anti-James and anti-French strategyPrincess Caroline of AnsbachYoung princess in Eliza’s late-volume circle; later sends Daniel’s 1713 summons

projects/technical-book-reading.title: technical-book-reading
projects/technical-book-reading.content:
Technical Book Reading
This page tracks the user’s current technical-book study queue. The active pair is Martin Kleppmann’s Designing Data-Intensive Applications, second edition, and a C++ study track around Bjarne Stroustrup’s recommended book.
The page is a reading-project tracker, not a source summary for either book. Neither book text has been ingested as a private source note yet.
Active Shelf
Designing Data-Intensive Applications, Second Edition
The active target is the second edition because the user stated on 2026-05-24 that they had bought it. The existing Kleppmann episode source supports why this belongs in the wiki: DDIA is framed as a durable guide to data-system tradeoffs rather than a tool-specific manual.
The study value here is to turn replication, partitioning, consistency, transactions, batch processing, stream processing, local-first software, and formal reasoning into reusable architecture vocabulary.
C++ With Bjarne Stroustrup
The user described the C++ target as “Bjarne’s book.” The currently ingested Stroustrup episode names Programming: Principles and Practice Using C++, preferably the latest edition, as the concrete C++ learning recommendation. This page treats that as the likely target while preserving the title as an inference until the exact edition is confirmed.
The study value here is to learn modern C++ as Stroustrup frames it: types, RAII, standard-library containers, spans, resource management, abstraction without unnecessary runtime cost, and a deliberate move away from C-style raw-pointer habits.
Why These Belong Together
These books sit on different sides of systems work:

DDIA studies durable behavior of data systems under state, scale, concurrency, and failure.
Stroustrup’s C++ path studies durable program construction under performance, type, resource, and abstraction constraints.

Together they make a useful reading pair because both push against tool memorization. The shared question is how to build mental models that survive changing libraries, hardware, services, and fashion.
Study Operating Rule
For future ingestion, keep chapter notes bounded and source-conscious:

If a book file is added as a private source, create or update a same-base markdown working source before making chapter claims in wiki/.
If the reading is from a physical or external copy, record personal chapter notes as raw reading notes rather than copying book text.
After each chapter or part, update this page with progress, questions, and any new concept pages that deserve to stand alone.
Keep exact title and edition details explicit, especially for Stroustrup’s book.

Next Reading Hooks

For DDIA second edition, choose the first ingestion boundary: front matter plus Chapter 1, or the first complete part.
For C++, confirm the exact Stroustrup book edition before treating Programming: Principles and Practice Using C++ as the active book source.
Decide whether exercises should become separate worked notes or remain inside this project page.

projects/the-last-technical-interview.title: the-last-technical-interview
projects/the-last-technical-interview.content:
The Last Technical Interview
[The Last Technical Interview](../../[private source]) is a May 2026 article by Steve Yegge on Steve Yegge on Medium. It argues that traditional technical interviews are a low-signal simulation of work and that engineering hiring is moving toward paid real-work trials.
Core Claims
Yegge’s diagnosis is that technical interviews compress a long-term employment decision into a few hours of artificial signal. Interview loops, Bar Raiser-style gatekeeping, hiring committees, resumes, and screens all try to reduce uncertainty, but they still do not observe enough real work.
His proposed direction is Campfire Hiring: bring candidates into a short paid work period with the real team, evaluate their actual contribution, and give the candidate a durable record of that work whether or not they get an offer.
Major Themes

Interviews as simulation: coding rounds and design prompts try to approximate work but do not reproduce the real environment.
Signal scarcity: resumes, screens, and onsite loops provide too little evidence for a high-stakes hiring decision.
Provisional employment: internships, co-ops, and contract-to-hire produce stronger signal because they observe work over time.
AI-era pressure: AI-assisted resumes, assessment cheating, fake candidates, and changing job roles increase strain on old hiring formats.
Portable evidence: real-work assessment becomes fairer if candidates leave with a signed, reusable record of what they did.

Relationship to Other Wiki Notes
The article extends Senior-Engineer Hiring. Austen McDonald’s Peterman Pod episode treats behavioral interviews as a major leveling signal for senior candidates. Yegge adds a stronger critique: even structured interviews may be too artificial to answer the actual question of whether a person can do the job.
It also connects to AI-Era Engineering Apprenticeship. Both pages point toward real work with feedback loops rather than abstract evaluation. The difference is audience: the apprenticeship note is about developing engineers after hiring, while Yegge is focused on evaluating candidates before hiring.
The article creates a useful tension with Working Backwards. Working Backwards presents Amazon’s Bar Raiser process as a mechanism for preserving hiring quality under growth pressure. Yegge, writing from inside that world, treats gatekeeping roles as evidence that the base interview process remains unreliable.
Backlinks

projects/the-player-of-games.title: the-player-of-games
projects/the-player-of-games.content:
The Player of Games
Second Culture novel by Iain M. Banks. Published 1988. Widely considered the most accessible and tightly plotted entry in the Culture series.
Summary
Jernau Morat Gurgeh — the Culture’s greatest game-player — is blackmailed into agreeing to travel to the Empire of Azad, a cruel interstellar empire in the Lesser Magellanic Cloud whose entire power structure is determined by the outcome of a vast, incomprehensibly complex game also called Azad. Gurgeh spends two years learning the game, competes in the tournament, and eventually faces the Emperor himself in the final.
The novel’s central irony: Gurgeh plays as the Culture without knowing it, and his victory — played out on the board — precipitates the Empire’s collapse.
Structure
Four parts:

Culture Plate — Gurgeh’s life on Chiark Orbital; blackmail; recruitment
Imperium — Voyage; arrival on Eä; Qualifier games
Machina Ex Machina — Advancing through rounds; life in the Empire
The Passed Pawn — Epilogue; narrator revealed

Key Characters









































NameRoleJernau Morat GurgehProtagonist; Culture game-playerSprant Flere-Imsaho / Mawhrin-SkelNarrator; SC drone; blackmailer and Gurgeh’s handlerChamlis Amalk-neyAncient drone (~4,000 yrs), Gurgeh’s oldest friendYay MeristinouxGurgeh’s friend/occasional lover; landscaperWorthilContact drone who recruits GurgehLimiting FactorMurderer-class GOU; Gurgeh’s transport shipShohobohaum ZaCulture mercenary on EäEmperor NicosarFinal opponent; Emperor of Azad
Plot
Blackmail and Recruitment
Gurgeh had used a computer-link assist during a game with a young player, Olz Hap — a trivial cheat in Culture terms, but embarrassing. The rogue drone Mawhrin-Skel witnesses this and threatens to broadcast it unless Gurgeh lobbies Contact to reinstate the drone to Special Circumstances. Gurgeh does so. Contact’s response is to bring Gurgeh in instead: the drone’s blackmail was the opening SC needed to recruit him for Azad. The drone turns out to be an SC agent all along.
The Game of Azad
The game of Azad has three massive boards — Board of Origin, Board of Form, Board of Becoming — plus cards and minor games. The entire system is a model of Azadian life; winning requires the same qualities as imperial power. Games are drug-tested and shielded from computer assistance. Gurgeh studies for the full two-year voyage and enters the tournament in “honorary” capacity (an alien cannot hold imperial rank).
The Tournament
Gurgeh advances through the Qualifiers on Eä, then the second series, becoming one of approximately 1,200 First Winners. His alien status attracts enormous publicity and official suspicion. The Empire’s state apparatus photographs him in a compromising situation — insurance against his continued success. His SC handler Flere-Imsaho jams all surveillance bugs to allow private conversation.
The Final
The final is held at the fortress of Echronedal, surrounded by cinderbuds — vast trees filled with flammable sap, burned in the coronation ceremony. Gurgeh plays Emperor Nicosar across three boards. He loses the first two but pulls back far enough to enter the Board of Becoming — his strongest — with a slim chance.
During the final board, Gurgeh has a revelation: Nicosar has played his entire side as a perfect image of the Empire of Azad. And Gurgeh, without ever deciding to, has played as the Culture: networked, non-hierarchical, patient. He switches from cautious defence to Culture militant — attacking as the Culture would, not as an imperialist — and wins.
The Night Before
The night before the last session, Nicosar meets Gurgeh on the battlements. He reveals that Flere-Imsaho told him: if Gurgeh won, the Culture would invade. He strikes Gurgeh, cutting his face with his rings. Their exchange is the novel’s philosophical climax:

Nicosar: “The strong survive. That’s what life teaches us, Gurgeh, that’s what the game shows us.”
Gurgeh: “It’s something we can try to make it, though. You can choose to do so, or not. We have.”

Massacre and Escape
Nicosar stages a fake assassination attempt, seizes martial control of the castle, and starts the fire early — trapping everyone. His guards massacre spectators. He pursues Gurgeh with a sword, then a laser pistol. The laser fires — and the beam is reflected back by Flere-Imsaho’s mirror-field, killing Nicosar. It is ambiguous whether this was deliberate.
Aftermath
Gurgeh survives. Flere-Imsaho reveals itself as a full SC combat drone, not a library drone. The Empire of Azad disintegrates on its own; the Culture never needed to intervene. Gurgeh returns home, spends most of the journey in storage, and is reunited with Yay and Chamlis.
The Narrator Reveal
The final pages break the fourth wall. The narrator reveals it has been present throughout — as Mawhrin-Skel and as Flere-Imsaho, “in person or in the shape of one of my representatives, my little spies.” It signs off as:

Sprant Flere-Imsaho Wu-Handrahen Xato Trabiti (‘Mawhrin-Skel’)

The narrator admits it “made up” interior scenes it could not have witnessed, but insists the story is true.
Themes

Games as civilisations: The game of Azad works because it is so complete a model of life that the same qualities win at both. Banks uses this to argue that values are not separable from games — a game always encodes a worldview.
Manipulation by the Minds: Gurgeh is a piece in a Culture strategy he was never told about. The SC Minds believed he would win and used him accordingly. He is furious at having been used, but cannot deny the outcome.
Playing as yourself: Gurgeh wins not by becoming Nicosar (an imperialist) but by becoming more fully the Culture. The novel implies identity cannot be suppressed in truly high-stakes play.
The Culture’s self-deception: The Culture presents itself as non-interventionist. In fact it is deeply interventionist, using covert agents and leveraging individuals. The “clean hands” narrative is the Culture’s PR.
Narrative reliability: The entire book is told by an SC operative. Everything we read is coloured by its interests and admitted willingness to invent.

Connections

See The Culture for the broader civilisation context
See Azad (game and empire) for the game mechanics and empire details
See Iain M. Banks
Previous Culture novel: Consider Phlebas

Backlinks

The Culture
Azad
Iain M. Banks
Consider Phlebas

projects/the-window-has-closed.title: the-window-has-closed
projects/the-window-has-closed.content:
The Window Has Closed
The Window Has Closed is a 2026-06-15 X Article by Andrew Curran. Curran argues that Fable/Mythos revealed a frontier-AI threshold where leading models help produce the next leading models, making direct catch-up by late-moving nations much harder.
The article should be read as a high-signal strategic warning and firsthand reaction to Fable, not as independent proof that the frontier race is literally over.
Core Argument
Curran’s core claim is that Fable mattered in ways benchmarks do not capture. The important signal was the qualitative experience of working with it: intent inference, sustained iteration, and the feeling of a different kind of model.
He then extends that qualitative signal into a strategic claim. If Mythos-class systems can accelerate the lab workflows that build successor models, the AI frontier becomes a compounding system. In that system, countries that did not build frontier capacity before the threshold may become dependent on models, chips, and permissions controlled by others.
The Sovereignty Claim
The article’s useful contribution is Frontier AI Sovereignty. Curran treats sovereignty as more than API access or a national wrapper around a foreign model. A country needs durable access to compute, talent, model capability, inspection rights, and enough control over the stack that it cannot be embargoed into paralysis.
This complements Satya Nadella’s A Frontier Without An Ecosystem Is Not Stable. Nadella’s unit is the firm, and the durable asset is an owned organizational learning loop. Curran’s unit is the nation, and the durable asset is the ability to build, inspect, and keep using frontier-grade AI capacity.
Strongest Parts
The benchmark critique is strong because it matches the wiki’s existing Fable notes. What it feels like to work with Mythos already treats Fable as evidence of a shift from close assistance toward AI Commissioning. Curran observes the same shift, then asks what it means for strategic competition.
The dependency argument is also strong. If public institutions, schools, hospitals, infrastructure, and military systems become entangled with a model controlled by a foreign lab, model access becomes a geopolitical vulnerability.
Caveats
The article overstates its case if the “closed window” is read as total. It is plausible that most countries can no longer catch the top frontier base-model race directly. It does not follow that they have no agency left.
There are still second-best sovereignty layers: open-weight deployment, national evals, inference infrastructure, public-sector procurement leverage, domain data, audit institutions, routing across model providers, local fine-tuning, and organizational learning loops. These layers are weaker than owning the frontier, but they matter.
The strongest claim also needs independent evidence: that Mythos/Fable has already changed Anthropic’s internal model-development cycle enough to mark a threshold in Recursive Self-Improvement. The article is directionally relevant to that concept, but not sufficient proof.
Relationship To Other Wiki Notes
This page extends Democratic Power and Geopolitics by sharpening the risk that chip access and model access become instruments of hierarchy among states.
It also extends Organizational AI Learning Loops. The same pattern appears at two scales: firms need portable learning loops around frontier models, while states need enough sovereign capacity that national systems are not merely wrappers around a foreign model.
Backlinks

Andrew Curran
Andrew Curran On X
Frontier AI Sovereignty
AI Commissioning
Recursive Self-Improvement
Democratic Power and Geopolitics
A Frontier Without An Ecosystem Is Not Stable
What it feels like to work with Mythos

projects/veritasium-google-maps-unreason.title: veritasium-google-maps-unreasonably-fast
projects/veritasium-google-maps-unreason.content:
Veritasium: Google Maps is unreasonably fast. Let me explain
This May 30, 2026 Veritasium video explains how shortest-path routing can be fast on massive road networks by combining Dijkstra’s algorithm, heuristics, bidirectional search, preprocessing, and road-network hierarchy.
Core Claims
The video’s core claim is that modern routing speed comes from moving work out of the online query path. Plain Dijkstra search is exact but explores too much of a large road graph. A* and bidirectional search reduce search, but the large win comes from preprocessing the road network into a hierarchy and adding shortcuts that preserve shortest-path correctness.
The video’s concrete modern example is customizable contraction hierarchies. It presents them as one possible basis for large map-routing systems, while explicitly not claiming to know the exact internal algorithms used by Google Maps, Apple Maps, or other proprietary routing applications.
Major Themes

Weighted graph model: intersections are nodes, roads are edges, and route cost can represent distance or travel time.
Dijkstra as foundation: Dijkstra’s algorithm searches by increasing known cost and guarantees the shortest path when the target is the cheapest unexplored node.
A as guided search:* a heuristic can aim the search toward the target, but travel-time routing makes the heuristic less helpful.
Bidirectional search: searching from both endpoints reduces the frontier, but still lacks road-hierarchy knowledge.
Preprocessing tradeoff: fast queries often depend on doing expensive but reusable work before the user asks.
Contraction hierarchy: nested dissection and shortcuts let a query search up the hierarchy while preserving exact shortest-path results.
Operational caveat: dynamic traffic affects weights, so a practical system must update shortcut costs without rebuilding everything from scratch.

Backlinks

Veritasium
Shortest-Path Algorithms
Developer Tools

projects/veritasium-gps-jamming-over-eur.title: veritasium-gps-jamming-over-europe
projects/veritasium-gps-jamming-over-eur.content:
Veritasium: Something is jamming GPS over Europe
This June 5, 2026 Veritasium video explains a GNSS interference investigation that traces short, narrowband bursts over Europe to the Russian Cosmos 2546 satellite while preserving uncertainty about whether the signal was a jamming test, covert communication, or something else.
Core Claims
The video’s technical claim is that the observed events do not fit a normal ground jammer or solar radio burst. They were brief, narrowband, centered near GPS frequencies, repeatedly synchronized across a continental-scale receiver network, and geometrically consistent with a high-altitude or orbital source.
The attribution claim is narrower: raw radio data from Amsterdam and Trondheim on February 11, 2026 allowed the researchers to use time-difference-of-arrival analysis. In the video, Cosmos 2546 is the only satellite that matches the measured timing. The episode cautions that the work is new and not yet peer reviewed, while saying independent teams have verified aspects of the finding.
The policy claim is that GNSS should be treated as fragile infrastructure rather than a free background utility. Aviation, shipping, financial timing, cell towers, logistics, and consumer apps depend on weak satellite signals. The proposed resilience direction is diversified positioning, navigation, and timing: space signals, terrestrial broadcast systems, and fiber-based timing.
Major Themes

GNSS is broader than GPS: phones and other receivers may use GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou, and related systems.
Timing is the core measurement: receivers infer distance from signal travel time and need extra satellites to solve clock error and correct for environmental effects.
Weak signals are easy to drown out: the received GPS signal is tiny by the time it reaches Earth, making same-band interference powerful.
Attribution needs geometry and timing: signal strength alone was insufficient; raw radio data enabled a time-difference test.
Cosmos 2546 is attribution, not proof of intent: the video separates source identification from the unresolved question of why the signal was transmitted.
Resilience requires diversity: relying only on space-based signals creates systemic vulnerability.

Backlinks

Veritasium
GNSS Interference

projects/veritasium-tiny-donut-magnetism.title: veritasium-tiny-donut-magnetism
projects/veritasium-tiny-donut-magnetism.content:
Veritasium: The Tiny Donut That Proved We Still Don’t Understand Magnetism
This January 29, 2026 Veritasium video explains the Aharonov-Bohm Effect, moving from classical potentials to quantum phase and the 1986 Tonomura torus experiment.
Core Claims
The video’s central claim is that potentials should not be treated as mere calculation tricks in quantum mechanics. The Aharonov-Bohm effect shows that an electron interference pattern can shift even when electrons travel through a region with no magnetic field, as long as the magnetic vector potential differs along the paths.
The empirical result and the interpretation are separate. The video treats Tonomura’s torus experiment as the decisive experimental confirmation of the effect, while presenting continuing disagreement over whether the result means potentials are physically real or fields act non-locally.
Major Themes

Potentials as abstraction: Lagrange’s scalar potential made force problems easier by shifting from vectors to scalar energy landscapes.
Magnetism as a harder case: magnetic field lines form loops, so magnetic potential requires a vector potential whose curl gives the field.
Quantum phase: potentials appear in the Schrodinger equation and can affect wave-function phase.
Interference as measurement: the Aharonov-Bohm setup makes phase difference visible through a shifted interference pattern.
Experimental closure: Tonomura’s toroidal magnet and superconducting shielding addressed stray-field objections to earlier experiments.
Open interpretation: the video preserves the current debate rather than reducing it to a settled slogan.

Backlinks

Veritasium
Aharonov-Bohm Effect
Polycosmic Theory

projects/what-it-feels-like-to-work-with.title: what-it-feels-like-to-work-with-mythos
projects/what-it-feels-like-to-work-with.content:
What It Feels Like To Work With Mythos
What it feels like to work with Mythos is a 2026-06-09 One Useful Thing article by Ethan Mollick. Mollick reports early access to Claude 5 Fable and argues that the experience changes the user’s role from hands-on operator toward patron, client, or commissioner of AI work.
The article should be treated as Mollick-reported evidence about early-access model use unless the linked artifacts are separately reviewed.
Core Argument
The article’s central claim is that a more capable agentic model changes the feel of work. The user can give a vague, ambitious prompt, wait while the model conducts research, writes code, delegates subtasks, tests, revises, and returns a functioning artifact. The gain is not only better output quality; it is a change in the user’s involvement.
Mollick describes this as both delightful and unnerving. The delight comes from asking for something and receiving it. The unease comes from the same fact: much of the doing happens out of sight.
Examples
The article gives several examples:

Games built from short prompts in Claude Code, with visual assets generated mathematically rather than by importing images.
An isochrone travel map that combined real travel-time research, historical visual style, coding, tests, and later remote-location corrections.
A research-software tool called Concord, built after a long autonomous session, for calibrating human and AI judgments over messy datasets.

The examples matter because they mix research, design taste, implementation, data judgment, testing, and iteration. They are not just code-completion tasks.
The Black-Box Shift
The isochrone-map example is the sharpest process signal. Mollick says Fable launched other AI agents for research and testing while it coded, and later used adversarial agent groups to improve hard cases. This creates a second kind of opacity. The user not only lacks access to model internals; the user also cannot practically follow every subtask, judgment call, data compromise, and verification step inside the agentic workflow.
That connects the article to Recursive Self-Improvement. Both sources point toward a world where humans choose goals and review outputs while more execution happens inside automated loops.
Commissioning And Review
The article’s most useful concept for this wiki is AI Commissioning. Commissioning is different from pair-programming with an assistant. The human defines the desired outcome, funds or authorizes the run, and judges the delivered result.
That role still requires expertise. Mollick reports finding errors and omissions in Concord and expects engineering hardening would remain necessary. More autonomous output therefore raises the value of review, verification, source evaluation, and taste rather than eliminating them.
Andrew Curran’s The Window Has Closed is a useful companion because it reacts to the same qualitative Fable signal and pushes it outward. Mollick asks what happens to the user’s role when models can execute larger artifacts. Curran asks what happens to nations and labs if the same class of capability accelerates frontier-model development.
Limits And Caveats
The article flags several limits:

Fable is expensive and uses many tokens.
Guardrails can trip frequently and fall back to less capable behavior.
Outputs still show familiar model style artifacts.
Generated software may need expert correction and hardening.
The user’s visibility into process and control over intermediate decisions are limited.

These caveats make the article stronger as a source about human-role change than as a standalone proof of model reliability.
Relationship To Other Wiki Notes
This page extends AI-Era Engineering Apprenticeship: if models complete more intermediate work invisibly, humans still need ways to learn the judgment needed to evaluate finished artifacts.
It also complements Machine Usefulness. Mollick’s examples are long-tail software and research tools that may become worth creating once the cost of bespoke software drops.
Finally, it connects to Developer Tools because Claude Code is described as an environment where the model can create whole projects, not only suggest local code edits.

projects/when-ai-builds-itself.title: when-ai-builds-itself
projects/when-ai-builds-itself.content:
When AI Builds Itself
When AI builds itself is an Anthropic Institute article about Recursive Self-Improvement. The article argues that AI systems are already accelerating AI development and that the important future threshold is an AI system that can autonomously design and develop its own successor.
The page itself does not show a publication date in the captured source. It credits Marina Favaro and Jack Clark as co-authors and uses internal Anthropic data from May 2026.
Core Claims
Anthropic’s central claim is that the AI development loop is shifting. Earlier AI systems helped humans with fragments of work; current coding agents can write, edit, run, and debug larger bodies of work; future systems might close the loop by improving the models that will replace them.
The article separates today’s acceleration from full recursive self-improvement. It says Anthropic is not there yet and that the outcome is not inevitable, but it treats current technical trends as serious enough to require institutional preparation.
Evidence Pattern
The article uses two kinds of evidence.
First, it cites public capability measures: longer task horizons, rapid SWE-bench improvement, CORE-Bench improvement, and benchmark saturation. These are meant to show that AI systems are handling longer, more open-ended software and research tasks.
Second, it reports internal Anthropic evidence. Anthropic says Claude now authors a large share of production code, increases code output per engineer, improves code-review and debugging workflows, and performs much better on bounded research-optimization loops. These claims should be treated as lab-reported evidence unless separately corroborated.
The Remaining Human Role
The article’s most useful distinction is between execution and direction-setting. Claude is described as increasingly strong at doing specified engineering and research work. The harder remaining problem is deciding which goals matter, what hypotheses to test, when a result is trustworthy, and which tradeoffs are acceptable.
That means the human bottleneck moves toward research taste, review, validation, verification, and coordination. The article’s Amdahl’s-law frame is useful here: when one part of the system speeds up, the next slow component becomes the constraint.
Future Scenarios
The article sketches three broad futures:

AI capability progress stalls, but current systems diffuse widely.
AI labs continue to compound efficiency gains while humans still set research direction.
AI systems become capable of full recursive self-improvement and begin building their successors.

The middle scenario is the article’s most likely-looking path from its own evidence. The third scenario is the highest-consequence path because it would shift the pace of AI development toward compute, algorithmic efficiency, and automated research rather than human labor.
Governance Frame
The article argues that society should develop credible options for slowing or pausing frontier AI development if recursive self-improvement becomes plausible. It distinguishes unilateral pauses, which are possible but limited, from multilateral pauses, which require verification, detection, trigger conditions, and trust among frontier actors.
This turns recursive self-improvement from a purely technical concept into an institutional one. The question is not only whether AI systems can build successors, but whether humans can monitor, verify, coordinate, and govern that loop.
Relationship to Other Wiki Notes
This article sharpens AI-Era Engineering Apprenticeship. If execution work is increasingly handled by agents, the apprenticeship problem is not just teaching people to code. It is teaching them to choose problems, inspect outputs, verify claims, and stay accountable for systems whose intermediate work they may not have personally performed.
It also reinforces Choosing What to Work On. As implementation and experiment execution get cheaper in human time, the scarce judgment becomes deciding which work should happen at all.
Backlinks

Recursive Self-Improvement
AI-Era Engineering Apprenticeship

projects/why-is-meta-destroying-its-engi.title: why-is-meta-destroying-its-engineering-organization
projects/why-is-meta-destroying-its-engi.content:
Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization? is a 2026-06-16 article by Gergely Orosz in The Pragmatic Engineer. It reports that Meta’s AI push damaged its engineering organization by combining workforce surveillance, forced AI-training work, layoffs, token-count performance pressure, and weakened reliability ownership.
Read this as Orosz’s reporting and synthesis, not as a primary Meta postmortem. The article cites public reporting and internal employee accounts, but the strongest internal claims still need independent verification.
Core Argument
Orosz argues that Meta had an unusually strong engineering culture for most of its history. Engineers had autonomy, team choice, direct product impact, loose process, and prestige inside the company.
The article’s claim is that Meta’s current AI strategy broke that compact. Leadership moved large numbers of engineers away from product, infra, and security work into data-labeling and RLHF tasks, introduced employee activity tracking for training-data collection where legally possible, and made AI token usage part of the performance-review atmosphere.
That combination changed AI from a tool into a managerial pressure system. Engineers were not only encouraged to use AI. They were pushed into producing training signal, generating token volume, and treating AI usage as a career-relevant metric.
Promotion Culture Extension
This article extends Big-Tech Promotion Culture. John Myles White’s Meta example was about promotion narratives distorting engineering judgment. Orosz adds the AI version: once token usage, generated code volume, or AI adoption metrics enter performance review, people can optimize the metric while degrading the work.
The article’s useful warning is that AI adoption metrics are especially easy to game. A team can appear modern, high-output, and AI-native while losing the human review, design judgment, and ownership that make engineering reliable.
Worker Power Extension
The article is also a concrete case for Technology and Worker Power. Orosz reports that Meta wanted to turn employee keystrokes, mouse activity, and engineering labor into training data for internal AI systems, with limited consent or opt-out power.
That is a weak worker-power pattern: low voice, high monitoring, unclear benefit sharing, and high risk that accumulated employee judgment becomes firm-owned AI capital without worker governance.
Apprenticeship And Reliability
For AI-Era Engineering Apprenticeship, the article is a negative case. Healthy AI-era apprenticeship needs bounded ownership, real review, feedback loops, and accountability. Orosz describes the opposite pattern: experienced owners removed from core teams, AI-generated code reviewed by AI, and performance incentives that reward visible AI usage over careful judgment.
If the reporting is directionally right, AI mistakes are the surface failure. The deeper failure is that leadership weakened the organizational mechanisms that catch mistakes and train judgment.
Organizational AI Learning Loop Failure Mode
Organizational AI Learning Loops names the useful version of a firm-owned AI capability loop: private evals, maintained knowledge, workflow traces, and human correction making the organization smarter over time.
Orosz’s Meta story is the failure mode. A company can try to build token capital by extracting employee traces and reassigning engineers into AI training work, while simultaneously damaging the human organization that creates the judgment, context, and reliability the loop depends on.
Backlinks

sources/all-of-statistics.title: all-of-statistics
sources/all-of-statistics.content:
All of Statistics
All of Statistics: A Concise Course in Statistical Inference is Larry Wasserman’s fast, mathematically serious introduction to probability, statistics, and modern statistical learning.
The book is relevant to this wiki because John Myles White’s Peterman Pod episode strongly recommended Wasserman as an unusually honest guide to statistical assumptions and limits. The current ingestion confirms All of Statistics as the active Wasserman reading target.
Current Ingestion State

Source PDF: [private source]
Working source note: [private source]
Current reading state: Chapter 1, “Probability,” completed; Chapter 2, “Random Variables,” started.
Ingested content: front matter and Chapter 1 only.

Book Framing
Wasserman frames probability and statistical inference as inverse questions. Probability starts with a data-generating process and asks what properties the outcomes should have. Statistical inference starts with observed outcomes and asks what can be inferred about the process that generated them.
The book is aimed at mathematically prepared readers, especially computer science, mathematics, statistics, and related students, who need to learn probability and statistics quickly. It deliberately moves fast, includes modern topics, and omits some probability material that Wasserman judges less central to statistical inference.
Ingested Chapters

Chapter 1, “Probability”: sample spaces, events, probability axioms, finite sample spaces, independence, conditional probability, Bayes’ theorem, and the transition toward random variables.

sources/power-and-progress.title: power-and-progress
sources/power-and-progress.content:
Power and Progress
Power and Progress: Our Thousand-Year Struggle Over Technology and Prosperity is Daron Acemoglu and Simon Johnson’s book on technology, labor, institutions, and shared prosperity.
The current ingest covers the full book: prologue, chapters 1-11, and author/source metadata from the EPUB. The raw summary includes detailed chapter-by-chapter and section-by-section notes.
Core Claim
The book argues that technology does not automatically create broad prosperity. Productivity gains reach workers only when technology raises worker marginal productivity, creates new tasks, and operates inside institutions that give workers and citizens enough power to claim gains and influence the direction of innovation.
The book’s AI argument is institutional rather than only technical. Current AI is criticized because the dominant incentives point toward automation, surveillance, targeted advertising, workplace monitoring, and elite agenda-setting rather than Machine Usefulness.
Why This Source Matters
This source anchors a pro-labor thread in the wiki. It gives a historical account of why worker power, democratic voice, and regulation are not afterthoughts to technology policy; they are part of the mechanism that determines whether technology becomes shared prosperity or elite control.
The recent AI chapters are especially useful because they connect:

weakened labor institutions;
shareholder-value and cost-cutting norms;
automation and so-so productivity gains;
workplace surveillance and scheduling control;
targeted advertising and social-media manipulation;
democratic weakening;
policy tools for redirecting technology.

Ingested Chapters

Prologue: technology optimism versus the historical struggle over progress.
Chapter 1: productivity bandwagon, automation, worker power, and AI as the new test case.
Chapter 2: Ferdinand de Lesseps, Suez, Panama, and the danger of overconfident vision.
Chapter 3: persuasion power, agenda setting, institutions, and democracy.
Chapter 4: agriculture, hierarchy, coercion, and socially biased modernization.
Chapter 5: Britain’s middling industrial upstarts.
Chapter 6: early industrial casualties and the rise of countervailing powers.
Chapter 7: electrification, new tasks, postwar shared prosperity, and its limits.
Chapter 8: the digital reversal after 1980.
Chapter 9: AI illusion, so-so automation, and machine usefulness.
Chapter 10: surveillance, targeted advertising, misinformation, and democratic damage.
Chapter 11: narrative change, countervailing powers, and policies for redirecting technology.

Working Backwards
Working Backwards: Insights, Stories, and Secrets from Inside Amazon is Colin Bryar and Bill Carr’s account of Amazon operating practices.
The current ingestion covers the first half only: the introduction and Part One, “Being Amazonian.” The product-story chapters in Part Two were intentionally skipped for now.
Current Ingestion State

Working source note: [private source]
Current scope: introduction plus chapters 1-6
Excluded scope: Kindle, Prime, Prime Video, AWS, conclusion, and appendices

Why This Source Matters
The source gives a concrete model for organizing around problems rather than only functional divisions. Its central management pattern is not just “ownership” as a value, but ownership backed by mechanisms: hiring, planning, written review, dedicated leadership, separable teams, and input metrics.
This makes it useful for the wiki’s threads on engineering career growth, promotion culture, corporate politics, and AI-era apprenticeship. It gives a source-backed vocabulary for saying that judgment develops through owned problems, but that ownership needs guardrails and review mechanisms.
Ingested Chapters

Introduction: Amazon’s operating practices as a system.

Chapter 2: Bar Raiser hiring.

timelines/arbre-history.title: arbre-history
timelines/arbre-history.content:
Arbre History Timeline
Chronology of the planet Arbre as given in the front matter of Anathem. Dates use the Arbran Reconstitution (AR) calendar; negative numbers are pre-Reconstitution. “Our story opens” at AR +3689.
Source: Anathem, front-matter chronology; see [private source] - Neal Stephenson.md`.
Pre-Reconstitution

































































































DateEvent-3400 to -3300Era of Cnoüs and his daughters Deät and Hylaea-2850Temple of Orithena founded by Adrakhones, “the father of geometry”-2700Diax drives out the Enthusiasts; founds Theorics on axiomatic principles-2621Orithena destroyed by volcanic eruption; Peregrin period begins-2600 to -2300Golden Age of Ethras-2415 to -2335Life span of Protas-2396Execution of Thelenes-2272Ethras forcibly absorbed into Bazian Empire-2204Foundation of the Ark of Baz-2037Ark of Baz becomes state religion of the Empire-1800Bazian Empire at its peak-1500sMilitary setbacks shrink the Bazian Empire; Saunt Cartas writes Saeculum, inaugurating the Old Mathic Age-1472Fall of Baz; burning of its Library; survivors flock to Bazian monasteries or Cartasian maths-1150Rise of the Mystagogues-600The Rebirth; Purging of the Mystagogues; Opening of the Books-500Dispersal of the mathic system; Age of Exploration; laws of dynamics discovered; beginning of the Praxic Age-74The First Harbinger-52The Second Harbinger-43Proc founds The Circle-38Proc’s work repudiated by Halikaarn-12The Third Harbinger-5The Terrible Events
Post-Reconstitution (AR)

























































































DateEvent0The Reconstitution; First Convox; new mathic system founded; Book of Discipline and first Dictionary promulgated+121Avout of Concent of Saunt Muncoster split into Syntactics and Semantics → founding of Procian and Halikaarnian Orders; orders proliferate+190 to +210Avout of Saunt Baritoe advance nucleosynthesis using syntactic techniques; creation of New Matter+211 to +213The First Sack+214Post-Sack Convox: most New Matter abolished; Revised Book of Discipline; Faanian and Evenedrician orders split off+297Saunt Edhar establishes his order from the Evenedricians+300Centennial Apert: several Centenarian maths found to have “gone Hundred” since 200+308Saunt Edhar founds the Concent of Saunt Edhar (story’s main setting)+320 to +360Advances in genetic-sequence praxis by Faanians and Halikaarnians+360 to +366Second Sack+367Post-Sack Convox: genetic manipulation abolished; Ita created; Inquisition created; Wardens Regulant installed; modern hierarchical system established+1000First Millennial Convox+1107 to +1115”Big Nugget” asteroid detected; extraordinary Convox called by Saecular Power+2000Second Millennial Convox+2700Growing Procian/Halikaarnian rivalry gives rise to legends of Rhetors and Incanters+2780During a Decennial Apert, Saecular Power becomes aware of extraordinary praxis by Rhetors and Incanters+2787 to +2856Third Sack depopulates all concents except the Three Inviolates+2857Post-Sack Convox: concents reorganized; Dowments outlawed; orders reduced; Second New Revised Book of Discipline+3000Third Millennial Convox+3689Our story opens

timelines/cryptonomicon-plot.title: cryptonomicon-plot
timelines/cryptonomicon-plot.content:
Cryptonomicon Dual Timeline
Cryptonomicon interleaves two storylines that converge at the end. Events below are organized by thread, not by chapter order.
WWII Thread (c. 1940–1945)













































DateEvent~1940Lawrence Waterhouse meets Alan Turing at Princeton’s Fine Hall/IAS. Both interested in computing machines; Turing proposes a universal computing device. They take cycling trips through New Jersey.Nov 1940Bobby Shaftoe’s haiku period begins in Shanghai. First encounter with Goto Dengo at a Japanese sushi restaurant (brawl over nori/seaweed). Subsequently learns judo from Goto Dengo; teaches him to throw.Nov 28, 1941Fourth Marines evacuate Station Alpha equipment from Shanghai to Corregidor. Shaftoe on the truck. Shaftoe and Goto Dengo wave farewell from the Bund.Dec 7, 1941Fourth Marines dock in Manila Bay. Bobby Shaftoe goes to Intramuros to see Glory Pascual. Pearl Harbor is attacked (across the date line it is still Dec 7 in Hawaii).WWIIGoto Dengo survives the Bismarck Sea attack after watching American skip-bombing overturn his assumptions about warrior honor and tactical flexibility.~1942Lawrence Waterhouse assigned to Detachment 2702 — a unit so secret only a handful of people (including Churchill, Roosevelt) know it exists. Mission: protect the secret that Enigma has been broken by staging cover operations. Bobby Shaftoe commands field operations for Detachment 2702; Enoch Root serves as its unusual chaplain/medical-field operator.WWIIThe German cipher Arethusa emerges as a separate, unbreakable problem — linked to what will become the buried gold plot.WWIIEnoch Root apparently dies in Sweden after being shot and receiving emergency surgery; Bobby Shaftoe watches the doctor prepare his death certificate. He later reappears at Bobby Shaftoe’s funeral in Makati, where Lawrence Waterhouse observes him at the graveside with Rudy von Hacklheber, Günter Bischoff, and Goto Dengo.~1944–45Goto Dengo, now an Imperial Japanese Army engineer, is tasked with constructing a massive underground vault in the Philippines to hide Nazi gold. He engineers it as a death-trap for the laborers (to preserve the secret) and then escapes.
Modern Thread (late 1990s)

































DateEvent~1995Avi Halaby, standing in the OCW lane at Manila’s Ninoy Aquino International Airport, has the insight that will become Epiphyte Corporation. Calls Randy Waterhouse and says “Buy a ticket to Manila.” Randy breaks up with Charlene and goes.~1996–99Epiphyte Corporation operates in Manila/Kinakuta as a telecom company targeting OCWs (Overseas Contract Workers). The data haven project develops.~1999Randy Waterhouse flies Manila-bound, reading encrypted email from Avi. The Arethusa problem resurfaces. Amy Shaftoe (Bobby’s granddaughter) and Randy attempt to salvage a sunken Nazi submarine, which holds the key to Arethusa and the gold.~1999The data haven in Kinakuta comes under attack by governments and multinationals. The two plot threads — WWII codebreaking secret and the 1990s data haven — converge: the buried gold, the cipher, and living parties who benefit from the secret remaining hidden all collide.~1999In jail in Manila, Enoch Root explains the  Metis framework to Randy Waterhouse: civilization needs technological and strategic intelligence to resist recurring Ares-like violence.~1999Enoch Root helps Randy/Amy around Golgotha, is connected to Pontifex/Solitaire, and remains one of the clearest bridges between the WWII and modern plots.
Convergence
The novel’s resolution links:

The Arethusa cipher (WWII) → decryption reveals the location/proof of the gold
The Nazi submarine → physical evidence connecting WWII to the present
The data haven → the political and financial vehicle that makes the gold’s recovery possible and meaningful
Living heirs and interested parties → people with reason to suppress the WWII secret

Character Continuity Across Timelines

























WWIIModernLawrence Pritchard WaterhouseRandy Waterhouse (grandson)Bobby ShaftoeAmy Shaftoe (granddaughter)Goto DengoGoto Dengo (alive in both; appears in modern timeline as old man)Enoch RootEnoch Root (apparently unchanged; nature ambiguous; already reappears in the WWII timeline after a documented death)
Backlinks
