timelines/idiran-culture-war.title: idiran-culture-war
timelines/idiran-culture-war.content:
Idiran-Culture War Timeline
Sourced from the appendix “The war, briefly (abstract of main text)” in Consider Phlebas (source, pp. 361–363). Dates are AD (Christian calendar version, extracted from A Short History of the Idiran War, original text 2110 AD).
Pre-War Disputes





































DateEvent1267 ADFirst Idiran-Culture dispute1288Second dispute1289Culture builds first genuine warship in five centuries (prototype; officially to test models against practice)1307Third dispute; first machine fatalities; war publicly discussed for first time in the Culture1310Peace section of the Culture splits from majority population; Anchramin Pit Conference → agreed withdrawal of forces (condemned by Idirans, acclaimed by Culture citizens)1323Fourth dispute begins; Culture uses proxy forces1326Culture’s War Council meets; more Culture factions renounce violence and split away
The War





































DateEvent1327War officially begins; Culture craft and personnel directly involved; Idiran-Culture War Conduct Agreement ratified1332Homomda join the war on the Idiran side — a tripedal species of greater galactic maturity than either side; had sheltered Idiran Holy Remnants during the Second Great Exile (1345–991 BC); joined because they feared the Culture’s growing dominance1333War Conduct Agreement amended: destruction of populated non-military habitats forbidden1335War’s second phase; Culture finally on full war footing; period of protracted struggleAfter ~30 years of warHomomda disengages from the war; Culture had grown too strong1367War in space effectively ended1375War on thousands of occupied planets officially terminated (conducted mostly by machines on Culture’s side); sporadic engagements continue for ~3 centuries on backwater planets
Outcome

Culture won. The Idirans never attacked Idir directly, and never formally surrendered. Idir’s computer network was taken over by Culture effector weapons and upgraded itself to sentience — becoming a Culture Mind in all but name.
Some Idirans killed themselves; others went into exile with the Homomda; others set up independent habitats; some joined the Culture, some became Culture mercenaries.
The Changers were wiped out as a species during the final stages of the war in space.

Statistics (from appendix)









































MetricFigureDuration48 years, 1 monthTotal casualties (incl. machines, medjel, non-combatants)851.4 billion (± 3%)Ships lost (all classes above interplanetary)91,215,660 (± 200)Orbitals destroyed14,334Planets and major moons lost53Rings destroyed1Spheres destroyed3Stars (significant induced mass-loss or sequence alteration)6
Historical Perspective (from appendix)

A small, short war that rarely extended throughout more than .02% of the galaxy by volume and .01% by stellar population… Nevertheless, the chronicles of the galaxy’s elder civilisations rate the Idiran-Culture war as the most significant conflict of the past fifty thousand years.

Why Each Side Fought
Culture: To preserve its purpose and clarity of conscience — its soul. Backing down from the Idirans would have destroyed the reason the Culture could justify its hedonistic existence. Despite being materially unthreatened, the Culture had to fight. (Source: appendix “Reasons: the Culture”)
Idirans: Religious jihad — “calm, integrate and instruct” all other species. They expected the Culture to back down. They comprehensively misunderstood Culture resolve. (Source: appendix “Reasons: the Idirans”)
The Homomda
A tripedal species of greater galactic maturity than either the Culture or the Idirans. Policy: prevent any single group from becoming over-dominant (a point they decided the Culture was approaching). Joined the Idirans in 1332 but disengaged after ~30 years when the cost became too high. Never committed full resources.
Backlinks

Consider Phlebas
The Culture

timelines/quicksilver-book-two-plot.title: quicksilver-book-two-plot
timelines/quicksilver-book-two-plot.content:
Quicksilver Book Two Plot
This timeline covers Book Two of Quicksilver, King of the Vagabonds.
1665

Jack Shaftoe, Bob Shaftoe, and Dick Shaftoe grow up as East London mudlarks around the Isle of Dogs.
Dick dies during an attempted anchor theft from a Dutch vessel. Jack and Bob are left with guilt, nightmares, and a practical education in theft.
After John Cole is arrested and sent to Newgate, Jack and Bob turn execution assistance into a service for condemned prisoners, learning how crowds, punishment, commerce, and theater fit together.

Late Summer 1683

Jack drifts through plague houses, fake soldiering, smuggling, and Continental war.
The Ottoman siege of Vienna draws soldiers, merchants, camp followers, artillery, horses, and looters east.
Jack joins the Christian relief of Vienna for opportunity more than piety.

September 1683

After the Ottoman defeat, Jack loots the Grand Vizier’s camp.
Jack pursues valuable ostrich plumes, captures the Turkish warhorse Turk, and follows the trail into a mine.
In the mine, Jack interrupts the execution of harem captives and rescues Eliza.
Eliza explains her Qwghlmian origin, Barbary captivity, harem education, and the unresolved powerful Personage from her past.
Eliza redirects Jack away from cheap resale and toward Leipzig, where luxury goods and financial instruments can be converted into more durable value.

Autumn 1683

Jack and Eliza flee through devastated Bohemia.
The landscape turns the Thirty Years’ War into present-tense social fact: ruined settlements, religious fracture, hunger, wandering people, and opportunistic violence.
Jack uses food, performance, and shared outsider status to extract route intelligence from vagabonds.

Winter 1683-1684

Jack and Eliza winter near hot springs.
Their sexual and emotional partnership deepens, while Jack narrates his past with Bob, John Churchill, Monmouth, Maestricht, and D’Artagnan.
Eliza learns that Jack’s apparent chaos is tied to guilt, class humiliation, appetite, and trauma.

April 1684

Jack and Eliza reach Leipzig.
Eliza sees how silk, plumes, coin, Kuxen, credit, and information become mutually convertible at the fair.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz appears as “the Doctor,” selling mining shares while preparing his calculus work for publication.
The Leipzig booksellers’ world links publication, reputation, priority, and the intellectual infrastructure behind the later Newton-Leibniz dispute.

Late April 1684

Jack and Eliza leave Leipzig with Leibniz’s caravan.
Leibniz’s mining scheme shows that geology, machinery, capital, rumor, and court patronage all have to work together before silver can become money.
Eliza keeps moving between commercial agent, sexual target, social performer, and potential correspondent.

Walpurgisnacht 1684

In the Harz mining world, Enoch Root gives Jack a practical lesson in quicksilver and silver extraction.
Leibniz’s machinery and Enoch’s mercury knowledge make mining a system of natural philosophy, labor, capital, and alchemy.
Jack’s Walpurgisnacht mine adventure turns quicksilver into hallucination, danger, and unstable symbolic material.

Summer 1684

Jack and Eliza approach Amsterdam, “The Place.”
Amsterdam appears as a conversion machine: warehouses, canals, ships, labor, VOC shares, bills, rumors, letters, and coffee-house talk all circulate.
Eliza belongs in this market world more naturally than Jack.
Jack leaves Amsterdam for Paris as a courier, trying to turn Turk and the plumes into money.

Winter 1684-1685

Jack moves through the Dutch Republic, Spanish Netherlands, France, Paris, and Lyons through horse brokers, Armenian coffee networks, bankers, couriers, and police attention.
Paris blocks Jack’s plans because the horse and luxury markets are controlled by class access and brokers.
Jack finds Monsieur Arlanc among Huguenot galley slaves and learns that an individual escape can endanger the whole chained group.
Exhaustion, illness, hunger, religious fear, sex, shame, and quicksilver imagery produce Jack Shaftoe’s Paris Hallucination.
Churchill recognizes Jack during a royal-military spectacle and prevents his immediate death.

February 1685

In the Hague, Eliza enters diplomatic and succession politics.
Jean Antoine de Mesmes, comte d’Avaux introduces Eliza to Mary of Orange, the Duke of Monmouth, Gomer Bolstrood, and anti-James actors.
Eliza begins to see that procurement of lead, saltpeter, gunpowder, shares, letters, and rumors can make rebellion and market speculation part of the same system.

April-May 1685

Eliza’s Amsterdam opera scenes convert Monmouth’s plans, French aristocratic performance, Sluys, VOC trading, d’Arcachon, and market expectations into political economy.
Jack is trapped by d’Arcachon-linked power in Paris and punished through humiliation and display.
Churchill gives Jack a narrow path out of immediate death, but not out of coercive power.
Jack returns to Amsterdam with Yevgeny and plunder from France.
Mr. Vliet and Mr. Foot convert Jack’s risk into maritime finance aboard God’s Wounds.

June 1685

D’Avaux escorts Eliza out of Amsterdam toward France.
William of Orange’s men intercept the movement, and William asks Eliza to become useful inside Versailles.
Eliza is caught between French state power and Dutch anti-French strategy.
Off the coast of Europe and North Africa, Jack’s voyage aboard God’s Wounds collapses into mutiny, Barbary attack, and capture.
Jack’s attempt to create a final heroic reputation fails because the crew cannot understand him. The book ends with Jack chained to a galley oar.

timelines/quicksilver-plot.title: quicksilver-plot
timelines/quicksilver-plot.content:
Quicksilver Plot
This timeline covers Volume One of The Baroque Cycle: Book One (Quicksilver), Book Two (King of the Vagabonds), and Book Three (Odalisque).
For a denser Book Two only timeline, see Quicksilver Book Two Plot.
1655

Enoch Root visits Grantham and observes the schoolboy Isaac Newton after hearing about him from the apothecary Clarke.

1661-1662

Daniel Waterhouse arrives at Trinity College, Cambridge after witnessing Hugh Peters’s execution with his father Drake.
The Earl of Upnor kills a Puritan scholar at Trinity. Daniel sees enough to know what happened but fails to bring the matter to justice.
Daniel meets Isaac Newton, another alienated Puritan student with a secret interest in natural philosophy.
Newton undergoes an intense Whitsunday repentance and begins the private habits of mind that later make him both brilliant and frightening to Daniel.

1663-1666

Newton performs dangerous optical experiments, studies sundials, colors, Cartesian analysis, and early fluxions.
Daniel spends the plague period among Drake’s London house, Wilkins and Hooke at Epsom, and Newton at Woolsthorpe.
With Wilkins, Daniel works around the Universal Character / Philosophical Language.
With Hooke, Daniel sees experiments involving microscopy, air, gravity, thermometers, animal anatomy, and measurement.
At Woolsthorpe, Daniel assists Newton’s prism work and hears Newton’s ideas about orbital motion and gravity.
The Great Fire of London kills Drake Waterhouse after King Charles II orders Drake’s house destroyed as part of a firebreak.

1670

Daniel follows Newton in London and discovers Newton’s connection to Monsieur LeFebure, the Earl of Upnor, and alchemical materials.
Daniel presents Newton’s reflecting telescope at a Royal Society meeting.
The Royal Society considers experiments, letters, instruments, anatomical reports, Hooke’s bubble level, and Wilkins’s Philosophical Language.
Daniel learns more about goldsmith banking, coinage, bullion, and the Waterhouse family’s post-Fire real estate ambitions.
Enoch Root demonstrates phosphorus at court, turning alchemical display into social danger.

1683

Jack Shaftoe, after a childhood among mudlarks, executions, and soldiering, drifts through Strasbourg and the Continent as the Ottoman siege of Vienna draws armies and opportunists east.
After the relief of Vienna, Jack loots the Ottoman camp, chases valuable ostrich plumes, enters a mine, and rescues Eliza from execution with harem captives.
Jack and Eliza flee through Bohemia. Eliza recounts Qwghlm, Barbary captivity, harem training, languages, and the still-unidentified powerful Personage from her past.

1683-1684

Jack and Eliza winter near hot springs in Bohemia, turning a survival partnership into a sexual and emotional entanglement.
Jack recounts his and Bob’s relationship with the Churchills, Monmouth, Maestricht, and the difference between soldierly honor and Jack’s looting instincts.
At Leipzig, Eliza realizes that goods, coin, mine shares, credit, and information can be converted into one another.
Jack and Eliza meet Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, who is selling mining Kuxen and carrying his calculus work toward publication.
In the Harz Mountains, Leibniz shows mine machinery and geological evidence. Enoch Root explains quicksilver’s practical role in extracting silver from low-grade ore.
Jack’s Walpurgisnacht hallucination and mine episode turn quicksilver into both mining technology and symbolic instability.
In Amsterdam, Eliza learns market reading and cipher correspondence while Jack works as courier and urban survivor.

1684-1685

Jack runs messages among Amsterdam, Paris, and Lyons through Armenian coffee networks, Genoese bankers, horse brokers, Huguenot contacts, and police surveillance.
Eliza meets d’Avaux, Mary of Orange, the Duke of Monmouth, and Gomer Bolstrood in the Hague, entering anti-James and anti-French politics.
Jack suffers the Paris hallucination, a grotesque vision of graves, skeletons, Huguenot galley punishment, priests, fishwives, and quicksilver-animated stone.
John Churchill recognizes Jack during a dangerous Paris military procession and intervenes before Jack is killed.
Charles II dies; James II becomes king of England.

1685

Daniel watches Charles II’s death and James II’s succession at Whitehall, while the court, physicians, and natural philosophers circle the event.
Eliza writes from Versailles as a governess, financial operator, and intelligence source, observing Maintenon, Louis XIV’s court, and French noble debts.
In London, Daniel, Hooke, Roger Comstock, Apthorp, and others discuss Newton’s Principia, Leibniz’s calculus, and the political heat around James II.
Eliza meets William of Orange near Scheveningen. William recognizes her value as an intelligence source and as a political instrument in the anti-French, anti-James system.
Bob Shaftoe’s Dorset account of Monmouth’s rebellion brings Abigail Frome and the Taunton schoolgirls into the slavery/punishment thread.
Jack is trapped by d’Arcachon-linked forces; Churchill saves him from immediate death but leaves him on a path toward galley slavery and possible escape.
Eliza leaves Amsterdam under French escort and is violently separated from her party.

1686-1687

The Exchange scene in London turns finance, insurance, commodities, Newton’s geometry, and Leibnizian objections into one public market of knowledge and credit.
Eliza manages assets for French nobles from Versailles using Amsterdam brokers and market channels.
She helps stage the false “Fall of Batavia” market operation, showing that rumor can move capital like military force.
Eliza studies Upnor, Abigail Frome, English slavery/punishment, occult networks, Enoch Root, and the genealogy/title machinery that might make her socially usable at court.
Daniel intercepts William Penn en route to William of Orange with letters from English elites, linking religious toleration, revolution planning, and Dutch strategy.

1688

Jeffreys abducts and tortures Daniel in the Star Chamber to extract revolutionary intelligence.
Eliza reports from Versailles on James II’s heir, English silver, insurance, French court dynamics, Liselotte, and the looming war.
Daniel is imprisoned in the Tower and confronts mortality, the body, the soul, and his debt to Hooke.
Eliza conducts an intelligence mission through the French war zone and Palatinate/Rhine crisis. Rossignol later reconstructs and partially recodes the story for Louis XIV.
William of Orange invades England. James II flees London, throws the Great Seal into the Thames, and is found by Daniel in a Sheerness tavern being abused by fishermen who do not recognize him.
Daniel chooses not to rescue James’s regime and rides back toward London and the Revolution.

1689

William and Mary are crowned.
Leibniz writes from Venice about William and Mary, Peter of Russia, war finance, Louis XIV melting silver, England’s emerging national debt, Princess Eleanor, and young Caroline.
Eliza writes from the Hague that the public story of her baby’s death is false. The child lives under a refugee cover story, and the father is Rossignol rather than d’Arcachon.
Eleanor and Caroline help protect the child temporarily. Eliza plans to move west toward London with the infant when strong enough.
In Bishopsgate, Pepys and Daniel’s Royal Society friends force Daniel toward surgery for his bladder stone. Hooke prepares the operation in Bedlam, leaving Daniel’s future survival as the bridge to his later Massachusetts exile.

1713

Enoch Root arrives in Boston, meets young Ben Franklin, and finds Daniel at the Massachusetts Bay Colony Institute of Technologickal Arts.
Enoch delivers Princess Caroline’s request that Daniel return to England to help with the Newton-Leibniz dispute.
Daniel leaves Faith and Godfrey in Massachusetts and boards Minerva.
Minerva is chased by Edward Teach’s pirate flotilla off Cape Cod. Dappa and van Hoek use captured pirates, feigned weakness, improvised ammunition, caltrops, carronades, and seamanship to survive.
Teach demands Daniel by name. Van Hoek refuses and nails his flag to the mast.
Daniel uses navigational calculation to confirm the pursuing schooner’s disadvantage; the ship grounds near Race Point.
Dappa persuades van Hoek that out-sailing Teach is sweeter revenge than fighting him. Minerva escapes into the Atlantic toward England.
